docs.title: Mini-Dialogue 1 — Shared Surprise Debrief
docs.text: Claude Sonnet 4.6 · 2026-04-14

docs.title: Dialogue
docs.text: Speaker 1 っていうかさ、さっきの……めっちゃびっくりしたんだけど！ Speaker 2 あいつ急きゅうに走はしり出だしてさ、何事なにごとかと思おもったよね。 Speaker 1 ほんとそれ。何なにか事件じけんでもあったのかと思おもったよ。 Speaker 2 マジで。びっくりしたわ。

docs.title: Natural Upgrade Audit (2026 / 30s–40s)
docs.text:
Register: Casual (タメ口) — close friends. Consistent throughout. Flow & Naturalness: ✓ Natural, logical, and colloquial throughout.
  • Arc is tight and energetic: S1 opens by expressing shared surprise → S2 names what happened and invites agreement → S1 amplifies with what they thought → S2 closes with emphatic confirmation. Classic mutual-surprise debrief, well-paced.
  • っていうかさ、めっちゃ、んだけど、あいつ、走り出してさ、何事かと思った、ほんとそれ、びっくりしたわ — all natural 2026 casual markers. The dialogue has good energy throughout and the speakers feel like real people reacting to something they both witnessed. Nothing textbook-like.
Verdict: ✓ No changes needed.

docs.title: Translation
docs.text:
Natural English:

Speaker 1: I mean, that thing just now… that totally shocked me! Speaker 2: Yeah… He suddenly just started running, and I was like, what on earth is going on. Speaker 1: Seriously, same. I thought something must have happened — like an incident or something. Speaker 2: Yeah, for real. That was shocking.

Literal English:

Speaker 1: Rather / I mean [topic], earlier's [thing]…… incredibly shocked [explanation] but! Speaker 2: Hmm……. That-guy suddenly started-running-and [narrative], what-on-earth thought right. Speaker 1: Literally-that. Something incident even existed-maybe thought [I tell you]. Speaker 2: Yeah, seriously. Shocked [sentence-final わ].

The Gap:

  • 何事なにごとかと思おもった and 何なにか事件じけんでもあったのかと思おもった — both use 〜かと思おもった to frame a past internal reaction. The か embeds an uncertain question inside the thought — "I thought [something like] 'what on earth is this?'" English "I thought [X]" is direct; the Japanese wraps the thought as an open question the speaker was asking themselves in the moment, which captures the real-time confusion more precisely.
  • びっくりしたわ — the sentence-final わ here is the masculine/gender-neutral emotive わ (falling intonation), not the feminine rising わ. It adds a personal, felt quality — "I was genuinely shocked [and I mean that]." English "that was shocking" is more distanced; わ makes the emotion feel personally owned.

docs.title: っていうかさ — Discourse Marker + Particle
docs.text:
っていうか = casual contraction of というか (or rather / I mean). さ = casual emphasis/softener particle.

  • "I mean / like / actually…" Topic pivot or intensified lead-in.
  • さ after っていうか adds a slightly more emphatic, conspiratorial energy — "I mean, seriously though…" S1 is building up to something they feel strongly about.

docs.title: さっきの — Adverb + Possessive Particle
docs.text:
さっき = just now / a moment ago. の = possessive/nominalizer turning it into a noun phrase. Predicate dropped.

  • "That thing just now / what just happened." Bare noun phrase as a topic.
  • S1 doesn't name what happened — both speakers witnessed it and shared context makes it unnecessary. さっきの…… with an ellipsis = "that thing [we both know about]…" The incompleteness is deliberate.

docs.title: めっちゃびっくりした — Intensifier + Suru-verb
docs.text:
めっちゃ = intensifier (incredibly / so much). びっくりする → plain past びっくりした.

  • "Was totally shocked / completely startled."
  • びっくりする = to be startled / surprised (intransitive suru-verb). Describes a spontaneous reaction — something happening to the speaker, not something they chose. めっちゃ intensifies to maximum casual degree.

docs.title: んだけど — Nominalizer + Conjunction
docs.text:
ん (explanatory nominalizer) + だ (copula) + けど (trailing conjunction). Despite the ！, still sentence-trailing.

  • "[I'm telling you,] it really shocked me — [you know?]" Emphatic explanation with implicit shared-reaction check.
  • んだけど frames the shock as an explanation and leaves it slightly open — S1 is sharing an experience and implicitly checking whether S2 felt the same. The ！ adds energy without closing the door to S2's response.

docs.title: あいつ — Pronoun
docs.text:
Casual, slightly rough third-person reference. Gender-neutral in modern usage.

  • "That guy / that person / them." Casual distancing reference to a third party.
  • あいつ = あ (distant) + いつ (person, archaic). More distanced and slightly rougher than あの人ひと. Between close friends talking about a mutual acquaintance, the roughness reads as casual rather than disrespectful.

docs.title: 急きゅうに — Adverb
docs.text:
• "Suddenly / all of a sudden / without warning."
  • Marks the action as unexpected and abrupt — no build-up, no signal. Critical to the surprise — the suddenness is what made it alarming.

docs.title: 走はしり出だしてさ — Verb Compound + Particle
docs.text:
Godan; Intransitive. 走はしる → masu-stem + 出だす (compound: to start doing / to begin suddenly) → て-form + さ (narrative continuer).

  • "Suddenly started running… [and then]."
  • 〜出だす as a compound suffix = sudden onset of an action: "to start/break into [doing X]." Captures the abruptness of beginning to run — not just "ran" but "broke into a run." Common: 泣なき出だす (burst into tears), 笑わらい出だす (burst out laughing).
  • さ keeps the narrative moving — S2 is mid-story, building toward the reaction.

〜出す as a compound suffix

One of the most useful verb-building patterns in Japanese. Attaching to the masu-stem, it signals the sudden beginning of an action — often with a sense of it being uncontrolled or unexpected. 走はしり出だす (break into a run), 動うごき出だす (start moving), 降ふり出だす (start raining). Once you recognize the pattern, you can productively combine it with almost any action verb.

docs.title: 何事なにごとかと思おもった — Set Expression
docs.text:
何事なにごと = "what on earth / what kind of thing." か = embedded uncertain question marker. と = quotation marker. 思おもった = past of 思う.

  • "I thought 'what on earth is going on' / wondered what the heck was happening."
  • 何事なにごとか = "some kind of [thing] / what sort of situation is this?" The か embeds an open question — S2 was asking themselves "what is this?" in the moment.
  • 〜かと思おもった = "I thought [uncertain question]" — captures real-time internal uncertainty more precisely than direct reporting. "I was thinking 'what on earth?'" rather than "I thought it was X."

docs.title: ほんとそれ — Set Phrase
docs.text:
Compressed from ほんとうにそれだ. Copula and adverb に both dropped.

  • "Literally that / exactly / same." Complete alignment with what was just said.
  • Standard 2020s casual affirmation — signals S1 felt the exact same way without needing to restate it. Economy of expression is the point.

docs.title: 何なにか事件じけんでも — Pronoun + Noun + Particle
docs.text:
何か = something (indefinite). 事件 = incident / event / case. でも = "or something / even [something like]" (scalar/listing particle in this context, not "but").

  • "Some kind of incident or something / maybe something happened."
  • でも here is the scalar/example particle — not the conjunction "but." "事件じけんでも" = "an incident or something like that." Signals S1 is offering an example, not a firm conclusion.

でも — two functions

(1) conjunction — "but / however" (でも、行いく = but I'll go); (2) scalar/example particle — "even / or something" (お茶ちゃでも飲のむ? = want some tea or something?; 事件じけんでも = an incident or something). Here it's function (2) — S1 is speculating "maybe an incident, or something like that." The tone of uncertainty (combined with のか) is the key signal.

docs.title: あったのかと思おもった — Verb + Nominalizer + Embedded Question + Verb
docs.text:
Godan; Intransitive. ある → past あった + の (nominalizer) + か (uncertain question marker) + と (quotation marker) + 思った (past of 思う).

  • "I thought [maybe] something had happened / wondered if something occurred."
  • あったのか = nominalized past question: "the question of whether X happened." と思おもった wraps it as S1's internal thought. Full meaning: "I was thinking to myself 'did something happen?'"
  • The の before か adds an explanatory/wondering nuance — "I was wondering [whether there was an explanation for this]."

docs.title: マジで — Adverb
docs.text:
マジ (from 真面目まじめ, now independent) + で (adverbial particle).

  • "Seriously / for real." Strong emphatic agreement.
  • Here not a question (マジで？) but a flat assertion — S2 is agreeing with S1's reaction. "Yeah, seriously [same]."

docs.title: びっくりしたわ — Suru-verb + Sentence-final Particle
docs.text:
びっくりする → plain past + わ (emotive sentence-final particle, falling intonation = masculine/gender-neutral personal feeling).

  • "That was shocking / I was genuinely startled."
  • わ with falling intonation = masculine/gender-neutral emotive particle, expressing a personal felt reaction. Adds a "and I really mean it" quality — the emotion feels personally owned rather than reported.

わ — two intonation patterns

(1) Rising intonation わ — feminine, soft assertion or mild surprise (そうだわ / そうかしらわ); (2) Falling intonation わ — masculine or gender-neutral emotive, adds personal weight to a statement (びっくりしたわ / 疲つかれたわ). In casual male speech, falling-intonation わ is common and signals the speaker is personally and genuinely feeling what they're saying. Confusing the two based on the written わ alone is a common listening pitfall.

docs.title: ～ちゃいなよ — Casual, Friendly Encouragement: 'Just Go Ahead and…'
入はいっちゃいなよ — "Just Go Ahead and Get In"
docs.text: **On this page**  - [JLPT Level](#jlpt-level) - [Quick Summary](#quick-summary) - [Core Meaning](#core-meaning) - [How It Works](#how-it-works) - [Grammar Analysis](#grammar-analysis) - [Japanese Mindset](#japanese-mindset) - [Nuance Comparison](#nuance-comparison) - [Register and Formality](#register-and-formality) - [Pattern Progression](#pattern-progression) - [Examples](#examples) - [Common Mistakes](#common-mistakes) - [Collocation Notes](#collocation-notes) - [Hidden Points](#hidden-points) - [Learner Stage](#learner-stage) - [See Also](#see-also)

docs.title: JLPT Level
docs.text: DeepSeek · DeepSeek-V3 · 2026-04-28 N3 Core  The input sentence uses ～ちゃいなよ, a casual contraction that bundles three grammatical pieces into one fluid, friendly nudge. At N3, learners move beyond recognizing individual contractions like ～ちゃう and start parsing stacked casual forms in real time.   N4 Base Learner knows ～てしまう as "completely / accidentally / finish doing" and its casual contraction ～ちゃう. 全部ぜんぶ食たべちゃった。 "I ate it all." (completion / unintended result) N3 Core Learner recognizes ～ちゃいなよ as a warm, casual urging: ～ちゃう + ～なさい (contracted to ～な) + よ. The bundle means "just go ahead and do it — no hesitation needed." 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 "Just go ahead and get in first." N3 Upper Learner understands the tonal range: ～ちゃいな can be lightly bossy, warmly permissive, or pragmatically efficient depending on relationship and intonation. They can distinguish this from softer alternatives like ～ちゃえば？. 早はやく着替きがえちゃいなよ。 "Just hurry up and get changed." (light impatience, still friendly) N2 Awareness Learner encounters regional and gendered variants of the contracted ～なさい → ～な form, including the ～なよ ending without ～ちゃう, and its use in Kansai-ben (～しぃな). はよ食たべぇな。 "Just eat already." (Kansai-flavored casual urging)

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text: - ～ちゃいなよ is a warm, casual way of saying "just go ahead and do it" or "why don't you just…" — it encourages action without pressure. - It bundles three elements: ～ちゃう (do it and be done with it) + ～なさい contracted to ～な (light command/urging) + よ (friendly emphasis). - The ～ちゃう part injects a sense of decisive completion: the speaker frames the action as something to get done with, which paradoxically makes the urging feel lighter and less demanding. - At N3, this pattern unlocks natural-sounding casual encouragement that sits between the stiff ～てください and the blunt ～て imperative.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text: Mental anchor: ～ちゃいなよ = "just go ahead and do it already — it's fine, I'm telling you." The speaker removes hesitation by framing the action as simple, complete-able, and obviously the right move. The ～ちゃう component does the heavy lifting: it tells the listener the action is a single, clean step — no lingering, no overthinking.  In the input sentence:   お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから、もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 Natural English: The bath's ready, so just go ahead and get in first. Literal: "The bath preparation is done, so — just go ahead and enter completely / get it done — I'm telling you." Context: A family member or close housemate has prepared the bath. The speaker is offering the listener first use — warm, casual, implying "don't wait for me, don't hesitate."   A simpler example:   遠慮えんりょしないで、食たべちゃいなよ。 Natural English: Don't hold back — just eat up! Literal: "Without holding back, just go ahead and eat it completely — I'm telling you." Context: A host urging a guest who is hesitating to start eating. The warmth of ～ちゃいなよ pushes past the guest's politeness without being rude.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: The pattern works by reframing hesitation as unnecessary. The speaker isn't giving an order — they're signaling that the path forward is clear, the conditions are met, and the only thing standing in the way is the listener's own reluctance. The grammar does this through a layered effect:  - ～ちゃう (from ～てしまう): Adds the nuance of "do it and be done with it." The action is presented as a single, complete-able step — something you can cross off the list. This is what makes the urging feel light: "it's not a big deal, just get it done." - ～な (contracted from ～なさい): A mild imperative. At full length (食べなさい) it's a parental "eat your vegetables." Contracted to ～な and bundled with ～ちゃう and よ, it softens into something closer to "go on, do it." - よ: The sentence-final particle that wraps the whole thing in friendly assurance — "I'm telling you, it's fine."  ### The Role of もう  In the input sentence, もう ("already / now / go ahead") is a frequent partner. It signals that conditions are already met — the bath is ready, there's no reason to wait. もう + ～ちゃいなよ is a highly natural collocation that pushes the listener past their hesitation point.  ### Real Speech vs. Textbook Form  The spoken form ～ちゃいなよ is a significant contraction from its textbook ancestor. Learners who only know the written forms may not recognize it in fast conversation.   Textbook / written form 先さきに入はいってしまいなさい。 "Go ahead and get in first." (Stiff, parental, feels like an instruction to a child — rarely used in adult conversation.) Natural spoken form もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 "Just go ahead and get in first." (Warm, casual, natural between family, close friends, or relaxed housemates.)   The contraction path is: 入ってしまう → 入っちゃう → 入っちゃいなさい → 入っちゃいな → 入っちゃいなよ. Each step strips formality and adds warmth.

docs.title: Grammar Analysis
docs.text: ### Function in Context  In the input sentence, 入っちゃいなよ does several things simultaneously:  - Signals readiness: The お風呂準備できてるから clause establishes that everything is in place. ～ちゃいなよ follows naturally: conditions are met, so action should follow without delay. - Removes the listener's social hesitation: In a shared living situation, there's often a polite dance about who uses the bath first. The speaker cuts through this by making it clear the offer is genuine and the listener should not defer. - Frames the action as trivial: The ～ちゃう component tells the listener: getting in the bath is not a production — you're not imposing, you're just completing a simple action. This is socially strategic. - Closes the negotiation: The よ at the end puts a full stop on the exchange. The speaker isn't opening a discussion; they're delivering a resolved position: the bath is ready, you're getting in, end of story — said warmly.  Without ～ちゃいなよ — if the speaker said 先に入ってください — the sentence would lose all its casual warmth and sound like a formal instruction, which would be strange in this domestic context. If they said 先に入って, it would be plain and direct but miss the "just get it done, it's nothing" nuance that makes the urging feel light.  ### Construction {#construction}   Word Type Base Form ～て Form ～ちゃいな(よ) Structural Note Godan verb (く) 書かく 書かいて 書かいちゃいな(よ) く → いて + しまう → いちゃう → いちゃいな Godan verb (む/ぶ/ぬ) 飲のむ 飲のんで 飲のんじゃいな(よ) む → んで + しまう → んじゃう → んじゃいな (voiced) Godan verb (る) 入はいる 入はいって 入はいっちゃいな(よ) る → って + しまう → っちゃう → っちゃいな (geminated) Godan verb (す) 話はなす 話はなして 話はなしちゃいな(よ) す → して + しまう → しちゃう → しちゃいな Ichidan verb 食たべる 食たべて 食たべちゃいな(よ) る → て + しまう → ちゃう → ちゃいな Irregular: する する して しちゃいな(よ) して + しまう → しちゃう → しちゃいな Irregular: くる くる きて きちゃいな(よ) きて + しまう → きちゃう → きちゃいな

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text: The ～ちゃいなよ pattern reflects a distinctively Japanese way of being pushy and kind at the same time — a combination that can feel contradictory to English speakers.  - Permission disguised as instruction: The grammar is structurally imperative (～なさい → ～な), but the communicative function is to release the listener from social obligation. In English, permission-giving ("go ahead," "feel free," "please, you first") uses soft, indirect language. In Japanese, the same function can be achieved with a light command — because the command is between equals in a close relationship, it reads as "I'm close enough to you to tell you what to do, and what I'm telling you is: stop being polite and just do the thing you want to do." - Completion as liberation: The ～ちゃう component encodes a cultural value: getting things done and moved past is desirable. By framing the action as "do it and be done with it," the speaker is saying: this isn't a burden, it's just a task — cross it off and relax. This contrasts with English encouragement, which often focuses on the positive outcome rather than the clean completion. - Warmth through grammar compression: The heavy contraction of ～てしまいなさい into ～ちゃいな is itself a signal of closeness. Over-enunciating the full form in a casual domestic setting would feel cold or stiff. The compression is what makes it affectionate — it's the linguistic equivalent of using someone's nickname. - Why "just" hides the grammar: English translations reach for "just" because there's no single word that bundles "completely," "go ahead," and "I'm telling you" together. The Japanese packs all three into ～ちゃいなよ without any of them being explicitly stated — the grammar itself does the work.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text: | Grammar Point | Core Meaning | Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance | Trigger Context | JLPT | |---|---|---|---|---| | ～ちゃいなよ | "Just go ahead and do it" — warm, casual urging | Friendly push. Bundles completion + light command + reassurance. Feels close and encouraging. | Family, close friends, relaxed peers — pushing someone past hesitation | N3 | | ～なさい | "Do it" — direct instruction | Parent-to-child, teacher-to-student. Uncontracted form feels stern or institutional in adult conversation. | Authority figure instructing a child or student; stiff if used between adults | N4 | | ～て | "Do it" — casual request/demand | Plain and direct. Can be soft or sharp depending on tone. Lacks the "complete it" nuance of ～ちゃう. | Casual requests between equals; can sound blunt or intimate depending on delivery | N5 | | ～ちゃえば？ | "Why don't you just…?" — suggestion | Softer and more tentative. The ～ば conditional makes it a suggestion rather than an urging. Less pushy, more "it's an option." | Suggesting without pushing; when the speaker wants to leave more room for refusal | N3 | | ～たら？ | "What if you…?" — light suggestion | Even softer. Often used when the speaker truly doesn't know if the suggestion is right. Can sound detached. | Offering an idea without investment in whether the listener takes it | N4 |  The key distinction from the closest lookalike, ～ちゃえば？, is that ～ちゃいなよ assumes the listener wants to do the thing but is hesitating — so the speaker pushes them past that hesitation. ～ちゃえば？ is more neutral: the speaker is offering an idea, and whether the listener takes it or not is genuinely up to them. Use ～ちゃいなよ when you're confident the action is right for the listener; use ～ちゃえば？ when you're just putting it on the table.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text: Casual お風呂ふろ準備じゅんびできてるから、もう先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 Natural English: The bath's ready, so just go ahead and get in first. Shift: The original. Natural between family members, close housemates, or relaxed friends. Warm, slightly pushy in an affectionate way. Polite お風呂ふろの準備じゅんびができていますので、どうぞ先さきに入はいってください。 Natural English: The bath is ready, so please go ahead and get in first. Shift: ～ちゃいなよ has no direct polite equivalent — the friendly-pushy nuance is inherently casual. The polite version defaults to a standard invitation with どうぞ and ～てください, which is warm and respectful but loses the "just get it done, stop hesitating" flavor. Formal 浴室よくしつのご用意よういが整ととのいましたので、お先さきにどうぞ。 Natural English: The bath has been prepared, so please go ahead. Shift: Formal register — appropriate for a ryokan or service context. ～ちゃいなよ is completely absent here; the urging function is replaced by polite deferential invitation. N2 — awareness only. Humble 先さきに入はいらせていただいてもよろしいでしょうか。 Natural English: Would it be alright if I went in first? Shift: The humble register doesn't use ～ちゃいなよ at all — it's the listener who might use humble language to ask for permission, not the speaker urging. Included here to show the social reversal. When the speaker is in the urging role, keigo doesn't apply; when keigo applies, the speaker is no longer urging.

docs.title: Pattern Progression
docs.text: ### What Leads Here  - ～てしまう / ～ちゃう (N4): The foundational contraction. Learner knows that ～てしまう marks completion, unintended results, or "doing and being done with it," and that ～ちゃう is its casual spoken form: 食べてしまった → 食べちゃった. Without this base, the ～ちゃいなよ bundle is opaque.  - ～なさい (N4): The polite imperative used from superior to inferior — parent to child, teacher to student. Learner knows that 食べなさい means "eat" as an instruction. This form is the source of the contracted ～な inside ～ちゃいな.  - Sentence-final よ (N5): The particle that adds emphasis and shared-context signaling: "I'm telling you." Learner already uses this for friendly insistence and new-information delivery. In ～ちゃいなよ, it's the final layer that makes the whole bundle feel warm rather than barking.  ### What This Unlocks  - ～ちゃいな (without よ) (N3 Upper): Dropping the よ makes the urging slightly more brusque or efficient — still casual, but closer to "go on, do it" than "go on, do it — I'm telling you." The presence or absence of よ is a fine-tuning tool for warmth.  - ～んじゃいなよ (N3 Upper): The voiced variant for む/ぶ/ぬ-ending godan verbs: 飲んじゃいなよ ("just drink up"), 遊んじゃいなよ ("just go play"). The same bundle, but the voiced contraction follows a different phonetic path and learners often hesitate on these forms.  - ～ちゃって (N2 — awareness only): A softer, more open-ended variant: 先に入っちゃって ("go ahead and get in" — said as a gentle nudge rather than an urging). The ～て form trails off and feels less directive than ～ちゃいな. Natural in the same casual contexts but even less pushy.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: 暇ひまなら、先さきに映画えいが見みちゃいなよ。後あとで追おいつくから。 Natural English: If you're free, just go ahead and start the movie. I'll catch up later. Literal: "If you're free, just go ahead and watch the movie and be done with it. I'll catch up after." Context: Friends planning to watch something together. One is ready now, the other is running late. The speaker releases the friend from waiting. 気きになるんだったら、本人ほんにんに聞きいちゃいなよ。 Natural English: If it's bothering you, just go ahead and ask them directly. Literal: "If you're concerned about it, just go ahead and ask the person directly and be done with it." Context: A friend is stewing over something someone else said or did, hesitating to confront the person. The speaker is pushing them past the hesitation — "rip the band-aid off." そんなに疲つかれてるなら、今日きょうはもう寝ねちゃいなよ。 Natural English: If you're that tired, just go to bed already. Literal: "If you're that tired, just go ahead and sleep and be done with today." Context: A partner or housemate is visibly exhausted but still puttering around. The speaker is gently insisting they stop and rest. 迷まよっててもしょうがないよ。好すきな方ほう選えらんじゃいなよ。 Natural English: No point agonizing over it. Just pick the one you like and be done with it. Literal: "Agonizing gets you nowhere. Just go ahead and choose the one you like and be done with it." Context: A friend is stuck in indecision — shopping, ordering food, or making a minor life choice. The speaker is closing the deliberation with a friendly shove.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text: ### Mistake 1: Using the uncontracted form ～てしまいなさい in casual speech  Learners who know ～てしまう and ～なさい but haven't internalized the contraction may produce the full form in casual contexts, which sounds stiff, parental, or even sarcastic.   Unnatural / stiff 先さきに入はいってしまいなさいよ。 "Go ahead and get in first." (Sounds like a parent instructing a child, or ironically formal — not natural between adults.) Natural casual 先さきに入はいっちゃいなよ。 "Just go ahead and get in first." (Warm, natural, unmarked casual urging.)

docs.title: Mistake 2: Using ～ちゃいなよ with strangers or superiors
docs.text:
The pattern is inherently casual and carries a light pushiness. Using it with someone you don't know well, or someone above you in status, can feel presumptuous or overly familiar — as if you're claiming a closeness you don't have.

Inappropriate (with a superior) 先せん輩ぱい、もう帰かえっちゃいなよ。 "Senpai, just go home already." (Overly familiar — sounds like you're treating your senpai like a peer or junior.) Appropriate (with a superior) 先せん輩ぱい、お先さきに帰かえられてはどうですか。 
<span class="pair-en">"Senpai, why don't you head home first?" (Respectful suggestion, appropriate social distance maintained.)</span>

docs.title: Don't Confuse With
docs.text:
～ちゃダメ (prohibition using ～ちゃ)

Learners sometimes mishear or misread ～ちゃいなよ as ～ちゃダメ because both begin with ～ちゃ (the contracted ～ては). But ～ちゃいなよ is an urging (from ～てしまいなさい), while ～ちゃダメ is a prohibition (from ～てはいけない). The structural origin is completely different.

Urging (～ちゃいなよ) 食たべちゃいなよ。 "Just eat up." (Encouragement: 食べてしまいなさい → 食べちゃいな) Prohibition (～ちゃダメ) 食たべちゃダメ。 "Don't eat that." (Prohibition: 食べてはいけない → 食べちゃいけない → 食べちゃダメ)

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text: The ～ちゃいなよ pattern frequently pairs with certain signals that frame the action as timely, overdue, or the obvious next step. These combinations are natural enough to learn as chunks.  - もう + ～ちゃいなよ: "Already" / "go ahead and… already." The most common collocation. もう signals that conditions are met and further delay is unnecessary. Together they mean "the time has come, just do it."   準備じゅんびできたし、もう始はじめちゃいなよ。 Natural English: Everything's ready, so just go ahead and start already. Literal: "Preparation is done, so already — just start and be done with it."   - どうせ + ～ちゃいなよ: "Anyway" / "might as well just…" Used when the speaker frames the action as inevitable or when alternatives have been exhausted. Carries a slight "no point overthinking" edge.   どうせ後あとでやるんだから、今いま終おわらせちゃいなよ。 Natural English: You're going to do it later anyway, so you might as well just get it done now. Literal: "Since you'll do it later anyway, just go ahead and finish it now and be done with it."

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text: - The ～な in ～ちゃいな is not the prohibitive ～な: This is a critical structural point that learners can miss. The prohibitive ～な attaches to the dictionary form (食べるな = "don't eat"), while the ～な in ～ちゃいな is a contraction of ～なさい and attaches to the ～て form via ～ちゃう. The two な endings are etymologically unrelated but phonetically identical, which can cause a split-second misparse in fast speech.  - Intonation distinguishes warm urging from impatient pushing: A flat or slightly descending ～ちゃいなよ sounds warm — "go on, it's fine." A sharply stressed ちゃいな with a clipped よ sounds impatient — "just do it already." The difference is entirely in delivery, not vocabulary. N3 learners should be aware that over-flattening their intonation can accidentally make them sound irritable.  - The pattern can sound gendered in certain contexts: ～ちゃいなよ itself is relatively gender-neutral in modern casual speech, but the full uncontracted ～てしまいなさい can carry a maternal/stern-feminine association when used by older speakers. In younger speech, both men and women use ～ちゃいなよ freely, but some older male speakers may prefer ～ちゃえよ (using the imperative ～ちゃえ from ～ちゃう) as a rougher alternative.  - The ～ちゃう layer also softens the command — paradoxically: At first glance, adding ～ちゃう to an imperative might seem like it would intensify it ("do it completely!"). But in practice, the opposite happens: ～ちゃう reframes the action as a single, clean step, which makes it feel less demanding than a raw ～なさい. 食べなさい is "eat (because I'm telling you to)." 食べちゃいなよ is "just eat up and be done with it — it's nothing." The completion nuance makes the action feel smaller, not larger.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: - What an N3 learner can reliably do with this pattern now: You can recognize ～ちゃいなよ when you hear it and correctly interpret it as friendly urging — you're not confusing it with the prohibitive ～な or mishearing it as ～ちゃダメ. You can produce it in familiar domestic contexts (with family, close friends, partner) where the social relationship is clearly casual.  - What still requires conscious effort at this stage: Choosing between ～ちゃいなよ, ～ちゃえば？, and ～たら？ in real time based on how pushy you want to be and how close you are to the listener. Knowing when the よ is needed for warmth and when dropping it changes the tone from friendly to efficient. Comfortably producing the voiced variants (～んじゃいなよ) without hesitation.  - What becomes natural later through N2+ immersion: Hearing the slight intonation shifts that tell you whether a ～ちゃいなよ is affectionately pushy or slightly annoyed. Using the pattern across a wider range of verbs and situations without pre-planning. Understanding when a native speaker uses the bare ～ちゃいな (without よ) and what that signals about the relationship in that moment.

docs.title: Reference Call Back
docs.text: If you've studied ～てしまう／～ちゃう at N4, you learned it marks completion or unintended results. In ～ちゃいなよ, the completion meaning is doing pragmatic work: the speaker calls the action "something you can just complete and be done with," which is what makes the urging feel light and encouraging rather than heavy. The same ～ちゃう that expresses regret in 忘れちゃった ("I forgot — oops, it's done") expresses liberation in 入っちゃいなよ ("get in — just do it and it's done"). This is a common pattern in Japanese: the same grammar can encode opposite emotional tones depending on whether the "completion" is desired or undesired.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: ### Same Family Related patterns within the same grammatical or functional family (casual urging, contractions of ～てしまう + imperative/suggestive forms): - ～ちゃえば？ — "Why don't you just…?" — Softer suggestion using the conditional - ～ちゃえ(よ) — "Just do it" — Rougher, more direct casual imperative from ～ちゃう - ～ちゃって — "Go ahead and…" — Gentle nudge using the ～て form  ### Commonly Confused With Structurally unrelated patterns learners mix up with this one: - ～ちゃダメ — "Don't…" — Prohibition using contracted ～ては + いけない (not from ～てしまう) - ～な (prohibitive) — "Don't…" — Dictionary form + な (not related to the contracted ～なさい)  ### Builds From Simpler patterns that lead to this one: - ～てしまう／～ちゃう (N4) — The completion / unintended-result contraction - ～なさい (N4) — The polite imperative, source of the ～な contraction - Sentence-final よ (N5) — Friendly emphasis particle  ### Unlocks Next Patterns that build on this one at the next level: - ～んじゃいなよ — Voiced variant for む/ぶ/ぬ-ending verbs - ～ちゃいな (without よ) — Bare efficient urging - ～しぃな (Kansai-ben) — Regional variant of the same urging pattern [N2 — awareness only]
