docs.text:
たら、ば、と、なら — nuance differences and when each is natural.

Add cards here.

docs.title: 謙けん譲じょう語ご I (Kenjōgo) — Show Respect by Lowering Your Own Status
1. Overview & Nuance
docs.text:
Extra Modest Expressions (謙けん譲じょう語ご) are a category of 敬けい語ご used when speaking about your own actions — or the actions of your in-group — in order to show respect to the listener. Rather than elevating the listener directly (which is the job of Sonkeigo), humble speech works by lowering the speaker, creating vertical social distance that flatters the listener by comparison.

The psychological intent is crucial: you are symbolically bowing through language. By choosing a verb form that means "I humbly do X" instead of simply "I do X," you communicate awareness of the other person's higher status — in age, position, or social role such as "customer."

In Japanese society, respect is often expressed through the concept of distance. This isn't about being weak; it's about being culturally intelligent. Using these forms demonstrates that you understand the social hierarchy and your place within it.

docs.title: How it differs from 丁てい寧ねい語ご
docs.text:
Form Purpose Direction of Respect Teineigo (-masu) General politeness; neutral distance Flat — simply polite Kenjōgo (Extra Modest) Lower yourself to elevate the listener ↓ Speaker goes down Sonkeigo (Honorific) Directly elevate the listener's actions ↑ Listener goes up

A simple way to think about it: -masu is polite neutrality. Kenjōgo is a verbal bow. If Teineigo is business casual, Kenjōgo is a pressed suit.

docs.title: 2a. Special Modest Verbs
docs.text:
Several high-frequency verbs have completely irregular humble forms that must be memorized. These are the ones you will encounter most often in real life — in customer service, business, and formal introductions.

Plain Verb Meaning Humble Form With -masu 行いく / 来くる to go / to come 参まいる 参まいります いる to be / exist (animate) おる おります ある to exist (inanimate) ございます ございます です to be (copula) でございます でございます する to do 致いたす 致いたします ている ongoing / continuous ております ております 言いう to say 申もうす 申もうします / 申もうし上あげます 食たべる / 飲のむ / もらう to eat / drink / receive いただく いただきます 知しっている to know 存ぞんじております 存ぞんじております 見みる to see / look 拝はい見けんする 拝はい見けんします

docs.title: 2b. The Productive Pattern: お + Verb Stem + する／いたす
docs.text:
For verbs that don't have a special humble form, Japanese provides a productive template. Three steps:

  1. Take the -masu stem. Remove ます from the polite form. 例: 書かきます → 書かき

  2. Add お before the stem. This prefix is a respect marker. 例: お書かき

  3. Attach する (or the more formal いたす) after the stem. 例: お書かきする → お書かきいたします

Plain Verb -masu Stem Standard Humble Extra Formal Humble 持もつ (to carry) 持もち お持もちする お持もちいたします 送おくる (to send) 送おくり お送おくりする お送おくりいたします 待まつ (to wait) 待まち お待まちする お待まちいたします 呼よぶ (to call) 呼よび お呼よびする お呼よびいたします

Sino-Japanese verbs use ご, not お

For する verbs with Chinese-origin nouns like 連れん絡らくする, 説せつ明めいする, 案あん内ないする, the prefix changes from お to ご, and you skip the -masu stem logic entirely: ご + noun + する／いたす.

例: ご連れん絡らくいたします = "I will (humbly) contact you." 例: ご案あん内ない致いたします = "I will (humbly) guide you."

docs.title: 3. Progressive Usage Ladder
docs.text: The following examples escalate in social weight. Notice how the same grammar is recycled across vastly different contexts — the grammar itself is fixed; what changes is who you're talking to.

docs.title: Level 1 — Strict / Formal (Interview, Ceremonial Self-Introduction)
docs.text:
Example 1 · Self-Introduction

田た中なかと申もうします。アメリカから参まいりました。 "My name is Tanaka. I have come from America."

Classic job interview opener. 申もうします replaces 言いいます; 参まいりました replaces 来きました.

Example 2 · Interview Statement

現げん在ざい、ABC会がい社しゃで働はたらいております。 "I am currently working at ABC Company."

おる is used in its て-form → 〜ており(ます), the humble equivalent of the progressive 〜ている.

Example 3 · Showing Respect for Expertise

お名な前まえは以い前ぜんから存ぞんじております。 "I have known your name for some time."

存ぞんじております is the humble form of 知しっています. Crucial in first meetings with someone famous or senior.

docs.title: Level 2 — Business Polite (Client, Superior, Customer Service)
docs.text:
Example 4 · Phone / Email

明あ日した、資し料りょうをお送おくりいたします。 "I will send the documents tomorrow."

Textbook example of the お + stem + いたす pattern. Standard in client email.

Example 5 · Offering Help

お荷に物もつ、お持もちしましょうか。 "Shall I carry your luggage for you?"

The humble pattern combines naturally with 〜ましょうか to make humble offers. Very common in hotels and stores.

Example 6 · Customer Service

少しょう々しょうお待まちください。担たん当とう者しゃにご連れん絡らくいたします。 "Please wait a moment. I will contact the person in charge."

Notice the ご + 連れん絡らく + いたす pattern for a Sino-Japanese verb.

Example 7 · Referring to Your In-Group (Uchi-Soto in Action)

社しゃ長ちょうの山やま田だはただいま席せきを外はずしております。 "Our president, Yamada, is currently away from his desk."

Even though Yamada is your boss, you use humble language about him to an outside customer. This is the uchi-soto rule in action — your boss belongs to your group relative to the outsider.

docs.title: Level 3 — Social Context: Humble Forms in Everyday Formal Speech
docs.text:
When does humble speech appear outside the office?

The full 致いたします register would feel stiff between friends. But 〜ております and お + stem + する appear in softer social contexts — introducing yourself at a PTA meeting, speaking to your partner's parents, or making a humble offer to a neighbor.

Example 8 · Meeting a Partner's Parents

東とう京きょうで英えい語ごを教おしえております。 "I teach English in Tokyo."

〜ております softens a casual fact into something appropriate for a respectful first meeting.

Example 9 · Polite Social Offer

タクシーをお呼よびしましょうか。 "Shall I call a taxi for you?"

Works when hosting older guests — more respectful than タクシーを呼よびましょうか, but not as stiff as business speech.

Example 10 · Expressing Gratitude

おいしいお茶ちゃをいただきました。ありがとうございます。 "I received some delicious tea. Thank you very much."

いただく is the quiet workhorse of humble speech — used constantly and perfectly natural even in moderately formal social situations.

Example 11 · Awaiting a Reply (Closing an Email)

連れん絡らくをお待まちしております。 "I am humbly awaiting your contact."

Uses て + おります (the modest て-form). Perfect for closing emails where you are waiting for a decision or reply.

Example 12 · Acknowledging a Superior's Message

そのメールは、もう拝はい見けんしました。 "I have already humbly looked at that email."

拝はい見けん is the humble version of 見みる. Shows you have paid careful attention to the superior's message.

docs.title: 4. The Keigo Hierarchy — Side-by-Side
docs.text:
Memorizing these triplets is one of the fastest ways to cement the logic of keigo. The golden rule: my actions → Kenjōgo. Their actions → Sonkeigo. Neutral / general description → Teineigo.

Meaning Kenjōgo (Humble — self) Teineigo (Polite — neutral) Sonkeigo (Honorific — other) to be / exist おります います いらっしゃいます to go / come 参まいります 行いきます / 来きます いらっしゃいます to do 致いたします します なさいます to say 申もうします 言いいます おっしゃいます to eat / drink いただきます 食たべます / 飲のみます 召めし上あがります to see 拝はい見けんします 見みます ご覧らんになります to know 存ぞんじております 知しっています ご存ぞんじです to exist (inanimate) ございます あります N/A to be (copula) でございます です N/A

docs.title: 5. The Mistake Monitor
docs.text:
Social Pitfall — The Uchi-Soto Rule

Humble speech is for you and your in-group (内うち) — never for the listener or their group (外そと). A common early mistake:

❌ お客きゃく様さまが参まいりました。 (lowers the customer — the opposite of your intent) ✅ お客きゃく様さまがいらっしゃいました。 (Sonkeigo — correct)

Conversely, when referring to your own boss while speaking to an outside client, use humble speech for your boss — because your boss is in your group relative to the outsider. This is counterintuitive for learners from hierarchical cultures that always elevate the boss.

Remember: use modest speech for your boss when talking about your boss to a client, but use honorific speech when talking directly to your boss.

Grammatical Pitfall — Keigo Double-Dipping (二に重じゅう敬けい語ご)

Do not stack humble and honorific structures, or apply the お〜する pattern on top of a verb that already has a special humble form.

❌ お申もうしいたします。 (申もうす is already humble — don't add お〜いたす on top) ✅ 申もうします。 / 申もうし上あげます。

Another classic error — applying humble speech to the listener's action:

❌ 先せん生せいはコーヒーをいただきますか。 (uses humble "receive/drink" for the teacher) ✅ 先せん生せいはコーヒーを召めし上あがりますか。 (Sonkeigo — elevating the teacher's action)

Grammar Pitfall — Using お〜する When a Special Verb Exists

If a verb has an irregular humble form, use it — don't invent a pattern-based version.

❌ お食たべします。 (invented pattern — unnatural) ✅ いただきます。 (correct special humble form for eating/drinking/receiving)

Social Mismatch — Using Keigo with Friends

Extra Modest forms are for formal settings with a clear social gap. Using 致いたします or おります with close friends creates a cold, yoso-yososhii distance.

Stick to casual or standard polite speech with peers.

docs.title: 6. Practice Drill
docs.text:
1. Translate to humble Japanese: "My name is Smith. I come from Canada." (formal self-introduction)
  2. Translate to humble Japanese: "I will send the report by email tomorrow." (to a client)
  3. Convert: 「今いま、会かい議ぎ室しつにいます。」(talking about your own location to a boss on the phone)
  4. Convert: 「駅えきまで車くるまで送おくりましょうか。」(humble offer to drive someone to the station)
  5. Spot and fix the error: 部ぶ長ちょうの鈴すず木きはただいまいらっしゃいません。(said to an outside client)

docs.title: 7. Answer Key
docs.text:
Click to reveal answers

Question 1 スミスと申もうします。カナダから参まいりました。 言いう → 申もうす for stating one's own name; 来くる → 参まいる for humbly "coming." Past tense 参まいりました is correct because the arrival is a completed fact at the moment of speaking. Crucially, drop the -san from your own name when being modest to outsiders.

Question 2 明あ日した、レポートをメールでお送おくりいたします。 送おくる has no special humble form, so use the productive pattern: お + 送おくり (stem) + いたす. Using いたす rather than する raises the register to business-appropriate. Both お送おくりします and お送おくりいたします are grammatically fine; the latter is more formal.

Question 3 今いま、会かい議ぎ室しつにおります。 いる → おる. The -masu form is おります. Used for describing your own location or state — common on the phone.

Question 4 駅えきまで車くるまでお送おくりしましょうか。 Classic お + stem + する pattern combined with 〜ましょうか to make a humble offer. The humble pattern works best when your action directly benefits the listener — which is exactly the case here.

Question 5 ❌ 部ぶ長ちょうの鈴すず木きはただいまいらっしゃいません。 ✅ 部ぶ長ちょうの鈴すず木きはただいま席せきを外はずしております。/ おりません。 Error: いらっしゃいません is Sonkeigo (honorific) — but Buchou Suzuki is your in-group relative to the outside client. You must switch to humble speech: おりません, or the more natural 席せきを外はずしております ("is away from his desk"). This is the uchi-soto rule at its trickiest — elevating your own boss to an outsider is a social faux pas in Japanese.

docs.title: Packaging Your Own Feeling or Wish as a Soft Inner Voice — N3 Grammar
1. Overview & Nuance
docs.text:
なーって (and its shorter variant なって) is a casual spoken structure used to package your own feelings, wishes, or desires as a soft internal quotation — rather than stating them directly, you let the listener hear your inner voice at a comfortable, low-pressure distance.

The core sentence for this guide:

今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエのところに、遊あそびに行いけたら、嬉うれしいなーって思おもってるんだけど。 "I've kind of been hoping I could go hang out at Tomoe's place this weekend, if that works out."

The speaker doesn't say "I want to go." They say "I've been inwardly going, 'that would be so nice…'" — a meaningfully different social move.

Core Rule

Use なーって to package your own feeling or wish as a soft internal quotation. You're not announcing it directly — you're letting the listener hear your inner voice at a safe, low-pressure distance.

docs.title: How it differs from related structures
docs.text:
Form Core Function Direction たい States a desire directly as a fact Speaker → listener (direct) と思う States a view or opinion Speaker → listener (assertive) なーって思う Reports a wish as an inner voice Speaker's inner world → shared gently かなって思う Reports genuine uncertainty / wondering Speaker's inner world → open-ended

A simple way to think about it: たい is "I want to go." なーって思ってる is "I've been sitting here quietly thinking it'd be so nice if I could go." The desire is identical — the social packaging is entirely different.

Why this matters in Japanese communication

Japanese casual speech — especially between close friends and partners — strongly favors framing desires as inner-voice reports rather than direct requests. なーって思ってるんだけど signals "I'm sharing this with you, not putting it on you." The listener can respond, affirm, or suggest an alternative without awkwardness. This indirect softness is not hesitation — it is skilled social calibration.

docs.title: 2a. What なーって is built from
docs.text:
なーって is a two-part structure:

  1. な — a sentence-final particle expressing a soft personal feeling, wish, or emotional color (distinct from the prohibitive な)
  2. って — the casual quotation marker, equivalent to と in quotation structures

Together they mean: "[inner feeling/wish]…" — is what I've been thinking/feeling.

The elongated なー (vs. plain な) is not decorative. It signals a held, slightly longing emotional state — warmer and more wistful. Shortening it to なって makes the same sentence slightly more matter-of-fact. Native speakers adjust this micro-signal unconsciously.

docs.title: 2b. Attachment rules by word type
docs.text:
Word Type Base Form + なーって Note Verb — plain non-past 行いく 行いくなーって Direct attachment to plain form Verb — plain past / potential past 行いけた 行いけたなーって Very common with potential past forms Verb — masu-form — ❌ Not used なーって attaches to plain forms only Verb — ない-form 行いかない 行いかないなーって Expresses resigned or reluctant inner feeling Verb — する／くる する／くる するなーって／くるなーって Direct attachment to dictionary form i-Adjective 嬉うれしい 嬉うれしいなーって い retained — the exact form in the target sentence i-Adjective — ない-form 嬉うれしくない 嬉うれしくないなーって Attach directly after くない na-Adjective 好すきだ 好すきだなーって だ is retained — unlike some patterns, it is not dropped here Noun 夢ゆめだ 夢ゆめだなーって だ required before なーって

The masu-form trap

なーって only attaches to plain forms. A very common learner error is treating it like a sentence-ender that works with any conjugation.

❌ 行いきますなーって思おもってる。 ✅ 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。

docs.title: 3. The Three Hedging Layers
docs.text:
The target sentence is more sophisticated than it first appears. It stacks three hedging mechanisms deliberately:

Layer Element Function ① 行いけたら Conditional softener — "if it works out" ② 嬉うれしいなーって Inner-voice framing — packages the wish as a private feeling ③ 思おもってるんだけど Ongoing (ている) + explanatory (ん) + open hanger (だけど) — invites a response

Together these turn a private wish into a gentle social bid. The speaker is not just reporting an inner state — they are opening a door. This is sophisticated, natural casual intimacy, not evasiveness.

Learn this as a complete pragmatic package

〜なーって思ってるんだけど is one of the most common ways Japanese adults make indirect proposals or invitations in casual speech. Learning it as a fixed social chunk is more useful than analyzing it piece by piece every time.

docs.title: Level 1 — Simple Inner Feeling
docs.text:
Example 1 · Self-reflection

最近さいきん、もっと運動うんどうしなきゃなーって思おもってる。 "Lately I've been feeling like I really need to work out more." Self-reflection shared as inner voice — not a complaint or announcement to the listener.

Example 2 · Wistful trailing off (なーって without 思う)

もうちょっとだけ話はなせたら良よかったなーって。 "I kind of wish we could've talked a little longer." Said after parting. Trailing なーって without 思う is natural when the wistfulness speaks for itself — the unfinished sentence is part of the emotional effect.

docs.title: Level 2 — Sharing a Private Thought
docs.text:
Example 3 · Long-held thought surfacing

そろそろ引ひっ越こしたいなーって思おもってたんだよね。 "I'd been thinking it might be time to move soon, you know." The んだよね ending invites shared acknowledgment — the speaker is drawing the listener into the thought, not just reporting it.

Example 4 · Tentative life decision

転職てんしょくしようかなーって思おもってて、今いまいろいろ調しらべてるところ。 "I've been kind of thinking about changing jobs, so I'm looking into things right now." しようかなーって signals the idea is still forming — invites the friend into the thinking process rather than announcing a conclusion.

docs.title: Level 3 — Indirect Proposal / Soft Invitation (the target sentence)
docs.text:
Example 5 · Soft invitation with どう？

ハルも一緒いっしょに来これたら楽たのしいなーって思おもってたんだけど、どう？ "I was kind of hoping you could come too — what do you think?" どう？ at the end makes the implicit invitation explicit without pressure. Classic soft-proposal structure.

Example 6 · The target sentence

今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエのところに遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってるんだけど。 "I've kind of been hoping I could go hang out at Tomoe's place this weekend, if that works out." All three hedging layers active. The speaker floats a wish, frames it as inner speech, and hangs the sentence open for the listener to respond naturally.

docs.title: 5. Nuance Comparison — なーって vs. Similar Structures
docs.text: Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional Nuance Best Used When JLPT なーって（思う） Inner soft wish reported as internal quote Wistful, warm, low-pressure Floating a wish or hope to a close listener N3 なって（思う） Same structure, shorter vowel Slightly more neutral / less wistful Same as above; な vs なー is emotional coloring only N3 かなって（思う） Genuine wondering / self-questioning More uncertain; speaker isn't sure themselves Open-ended wondering — not just softening a known wish N3 と思う Speaker thinks / believes X Direct and assertive; presenting a view Stating an opinion, assessment, or prediction N4 と思ってる Speaker is currently / ongoing thinking X More direct than なって; less inner-voice feel Sharing a current view or plan — less hedged N4 〜ものだと思っていた Had been thinking X was the expected state Retrospective; assumption that turned out wrong Reflecting on a past belief with mild surprise N2

docs.title: 6. The Mistake Monitor
docs.text:
Pitfall 1 — Dropping な and treating って as equivalent

Learners often drop な thinking it's optional filler. It is not — な is what transforms the content from a neutral thought into an emotional inner utterance.

  • With な: 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。→ "I've been feeling like it'd be so nice if I could go." (soft, wistful, inner-voice)
  • Without な: 行いけたら嬉うれしいって思おもってる。→ "I think it'd make me happy if I could go." (neutral, analytical, matter-of-fact)

Pitfall 2 — Defaulting to たい when なーって is more appropriate

たい directly states a desire as a fact. In casual intimate speech, this can feel slightly blunt where なーって would be warmer and more natural.

❌ (slightly blunt) 遊あそびに行いきたい。→ "I want to go hang out." ✅ (soft, social) 遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。→ "I've been hoping it'd work out to go hang out."

The desire is identical. The difference is how much social space you give the other person.

Pitfall 3 — Using なーって alone without 思う in writing

Trailing なーって without 思う works naturally in spoken Japanese when intonation carries the unfinished feeling. In writing, it can read as ambiguous or incomplete.

Ambiguous in writing: 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって。 Clear: 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。

なーって vs. かなって — the closest pair

Both are inner-voice quotations, but they answer different questions.

  • なーって: The speaker already knows what they feel and is sharing it softly. → 行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって ("It'd be so nice if I could go…")
  • かなって: The speaker is genuinely wondering and hasn't settled on a feeling yet. → 行いけたらいいかなって ("I wonder if it'd be good if I could go…")

If the speaker wants to go, use なーって. If they're not sure whether they want to, use かなって.

docs.title: 7. Formality Ladder
docs.text: Register Japanese Natural English What Changed Casual ⟦タメ口⟧ 今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエんちに遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってるんだけど。 "I've kind of been hoping I could head over to Tomoe's place this weekend." のところに → んちに (natural casual contraction). Core warmth unchanged. Basic Polite ⟦丁寧語⟧ 今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエさんのところに遊あそびに行いけたら嬉うれしいなって思おもっているんですが。 "I've been thinking it would be really nice if I could go visit Tomoe this weekend." なー → な (elongation is casual-only); 思おもってるんだけど → 思おもっているんですが. Soft indirect-proposal function fully preserved — ですが mirrors だけど as a polite open-ended hanger. Formal ⟦N2 awareness⟧ 今こん週末しゅうまつ、トモエさんのところに伺うかがえればと思おもっているのですが。 "I was thinking that, if possible, I might stop by Tomoe's place this weekend." 伺うかがえる is humble keigo for visiting — raises register significantly. 〜ければと思おもっている replaces the inner-voice quotation entirely. No longer feels like shared inner speech — feels like a considered proposal.

docs.title: 8. Practice Drill
docs.text:
1. Translate to Japanese using なーって思ってる: "I've been thinking it'd be nice if we could meet up soon." (casual, to a close friend)
  2. Convert from direct to inner-voice: 「一緒いっしょに住すみたい。」→ Reframe using なーって思ってる to make it softer and less direct.
  3. Translate: "I've kind of been wondering if I should quit." (Use しようかなーって to capture genuine uncertainty)
  4. Spot and fix the error: 早はやく会あいたいですなーって思おもってる。
  5. Choose the right form: Your friend asks if you want to come to a party. You're not sure you want to go, but you're considering it. Do you use なーって or かなって? Write a natural response.

docs.title: 9. Answer Key
docs.text:
Click to reveal answers

Question 1 そろそろ会あえたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。 Conditional 会あえたら (potential past) + 嬉うれしいなーって + 思おもってる. そろそろ ("soon / about time") sets the soft time frame naturally.

Question 2 ❌ (direct) 一緒いっしょに住すみたい。 ✅ (inner-voice) 一緒いっしょに住すめたら嬉うれしいなーって思おもってる。 The desire is identical. The inner-voice framing gives the listener more social space — less "I'm asking you for something," more "I'm sharing a feeling with you."

Question 3 やめようかなーって思おもってる。 Use かなって here, not なーって — the speaker is genuinely uncertain (wondering whether to quit), not softening a known wish. しようかなーって is the key form: the か signals the open wondering.

Question 4 ❌ 早はやく会あいたいですなーって思おもってる。 ✅ 早はやく会あいたいなーって思おもってる。 Error: なーって attaches to plain forms only. です is a polite copula — it is incompatible with this structure. Drop です entirely; たい is already a plain-form adjective that attaches directly.

Question 5 Use かなって — you are genuinely unsure, so the wondering form is correct. 行いけたらいいかなって思おもってるんだけど、まだわからない。 "I've been kind of wondering if it'd be good if I could go — I'm still not sure." If you already knew you wanted to go and were just softening the way you said it, なーって would be the right choice instead.

docs.title: なのに — Disappointed Contrast / That Said...
なのに — That Said... / Even Though...
JLPT Level
docs.text:
JLPT Level — Mid N3

なのに is an N4 conjunction at its core, but the emotional layering — disappointment, self-blame, mild reproach — and its use in internal monologue and soft complaint push it firmly into mid-N3 pragmatic territory.

Tier Pattern Description Example N4 — Foundation のに (plain) Basic "even though" contrast — speaker expected X, got not-X. Factual or lightly emotional. 勉強べんきょうしたのに落おちた。"I studied but still failed." N3 — Core なのに Adds な before のに when attaching to nouns and な-adjectives. Expresses stronger emotional contrast — disappointment, frustration, self-reproach. 休きゅう日じつなのに仕事しごとが入はいった。"It's my day off and work came in anyway." N3 — Extended ← You are here なのに + internal monologue なのに embedded inside self-aware inner speech — the speaker narrates their own contradictory behavior with wry regret. ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキ食たべちゃって…後悔こうかいしてる自分じぶんがいるよ。 N2 — Awareness くせに Same logical structure as なのに but sharper — directed at others, carries accusatory or contemptuous tone. [N2 — awareness only] 知しらないくせに口くちを出だすな。"Don't butt in when you don't even know anything."

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
At a glance

  • なのに flags a contrast the speaker finds disappointing, frustrating, or ironic — "even though X is the case, Y happened anyway."
  • It carries emotional weight that plain のに does not — the speaker expected or hoped for a different outcome.
  • In the input sentence it's turned inward: the speaker isn't complaining about someone else, they're narrating their own contradictory behavior with wry, affectionate self-blame.
  • The trailing …後悔してる自分がいるよ is a key pattern in itself — the speaker observes themselves as if from outside. Learn both together.

docs.title: Core Meaning
docs.text:
Core Rule

Use なのに when you want to express that something happened contrary to what the situation called for or what you expected — and that this contrast carries emotional weight: disappointment, frustration, irony, or soft self-reproach.

無理むりして来きてくれたのに、ありがとう。 You came even though it was hard for you — thank you. Literal: Even though coming was a strain, thank you.

~Context: Grateful acknowledgment — the contrast is touching rather than frustrating. なのに works for positive emotional contrast too, not only complaints.~

ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキ食たべちゃって…後悔こうかいしてる自分じぶんがいるよ。 I'm on a diet and I went and ate cake anyway… there's a version of me that definitely regrets it. Literal: While-dieting even-though cake ended-up-eating… regretting self exists, you know.

~Context: The input sentence. Wry inner monologue shared aloud — speaker observes their own contradiction with self-aware humor.~

docs.title: In Real Life
docs.text:
In Real Life

Situation: It's Sunday evening. リホ has been on a diet for two weeks and was doing well. She walked past a café, went in "just to look," and ended up ordering cake. She's now telling a close friend about it.

ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキ食たべちゃって…後悔こうかいしてる自分じぶんがいるよ。

"I'm on a diet and I went and ate cake anyway… there's definitely a part of me that regrets it."

Why なのに fits here: The diet is an active constraint — ダイエット中 establishes the context. ケーキ食べちゃって is the violation of that constraint. なのに is the emotional bridge between the two: "given that constraint exists, I did this anyway, and I feel some kind of way about it." The wry self-observation in 後悔してる自分がいるよ softens the regret into something shareable rather than a confession.

What makes this natural: She's not asking for comfort or advice. She's narrating her own contradiction — half-complaining, half-laughing at herself. This is exactly the register and emotional texture なのに is built for in casual speech between close friends.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
なのに is a compound conjunction built from な (the connective form of the copula だ) + のに (concessive contrast particle). Together they create a two-part statement: the first clause establishes a situation or expectation, the second clause delivers the reality that contradicts it — and the speaker's emotional reaction to that contradiction is baked in.

Layer 1 — のに (the contrast engine): のに connects two clauses where the second contradicts the first in a way the speaker finds noteworthy. Unlike けれど or でも which are more neutral, のに always implies the speaker expected or wanted the first clause to produce a different second clause. The disappointment is structural, not optional.

Layer 2 — な (the noun/na-adjective bridge): When のに attaches to a noun or な-adjective, だ becomes な before のに. This is the same な that appears in だから→なので. It's a grammatical requirement, not a meaning change — but it's the source of most learner errors with this pattern.

Layer 3 — Emotional register: In the input sentence, なのに is turned inward. The speaker isn't reproaching another person — they're observing their own contradictory behavior with a mix of mild guilt and wry humor. This self-directed use is extremely common in casual Japanese and is one of the most natural ways to share a minor personal failure with a close friend without making it heavy.

ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキ食たべちゃって。 I'm on a diet but I went and ate cake anyway. Literal: While-dieting even-though, cake ended-up-eating. The trailing …てしまう contraction (ちゃって) compounds the feeling — accidental, regrettable, slightly helpless.

docs.title: Real Speech vs. Textbook Form
docs.text:
In textbooks なのに often appears in straightforward two-clause sentences like 休日なのに仕事した. In real spoken Japanese two patterns are more common: trailing sentences that don't complete the second clause (ダイエット中なのに…) leaving the result implied, and なのに used sentence-finally as a complaint marker (どうして来てくれないの、約束したのに).

Form Example Note Textbook (complete) 休日きゅうじつなのに仕事しごとした。 Both clauses explicit — clear but slightly formal Spoken (trailing) ダイエット中ちゅうなのに… Second clause implied — listener fills in the irony Sentence-final complaint 約束やくそくしたのに。 なのに at the end of a sentence — pure emotional marker, no second clause at all

docs.title: Function in Context
docs.text:
• ダイエット中 establishes the active constraint. なのに flags that what follows violates that constraint in an emotionally loaded way — not a neutral "but," a disappointed or ironic "and yet."
  • ちゃって (contracted form of てしまって) compounds the regret — the eating happened somewhat against the speaker's will or better judgment. The combination of なのに + ちゃった is one of the most natural pairings for expressing this kind of mild self-directed irony in casual speech.
  • The trailing … (ellipsis) after 食べちゃって leaves the logical conclusion unstated. The listener understands "and now I feel bad about it" without needing it spelled out. This is a key feature of natural spoken Japanese — なのに clauses often trail off deliberately.
  • 後悔してる自分がいるよ is a distinct pragmatic move — the speaker frames their own regret as a third-person observation ("there exists a self that regrets"). This creates gentle emotional distance, turning a potential complaint into self-aware humor.
  • Without なのに: ダイエット中にケーキ食べちゃって would state the fact without the emotional contrast. The diet ceases to be a relevant expectation — it's just background. なのに is what makes the diet a violated promise.
雨あめなのに出でかけるの？ You're going out even though it's raining? Literal: Rain even-though, going-out? Mild surprise/reproach — the rain was a reason not to go. なのに signals the speaker expected the rain to change the plan.

docs.title: Construction
docs.text:
Form Result What Changed Verb (plain form) 食たべるのに Direct attachment — verb plain form + のに Verb (past) 食たべたのに Past plain form + のに — "even though I ate / did" i-Adjective 寒さむいのに Direct attachment — い retained な-Adjective 元気げんきなのに だ → な before のに Noun ダイエット中ちゅうなのに だ → な before のに Noun (past) 休日きゅうじつだったのに だった + のに for past state contrast

The な rule

な before のに is required for nouns and な-adjectives — it is not optional. Saying ダイエット中のに is ungrammatical. This is the single most common construction error learners make with this pattern.

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
English handles contradiction with neutral connectors — "but," "however," "although." The emotional weight of the contradiction is added separately through word choice or tone. Japanese packages the emotion into the conjunction itself. のに and なのに are not neutral — they tell the listener how the speaker feels about the contrast before the second clause even arrives.

In the input sentence the speaker is doing something very Japanese: sharing a minor personal failure through self-observation rather than direct confession. 後悔してる自分がいるよ — "there exists a self that regrets" — distances the speaker slightly from their own emotion, which makes it easier to share without seeming to demand sympathy. The listener can laugh along rather than feeling obligated to comfort.

  • Emotion-embedded grammar. なのに doesn't just describe a logical contrast — it pre-loads the listener with the speaker's emotional stance. When you hear なのに you know before the second clause arrives that the speaker finds the situation unfortunate, ironic, or mildly reproachable.
  • Trailing sentences as social grace. Leaving the second clause unstated (ダイエット中なのに…) is not vagueness — it's considerateness. The speaker trusts the listener to understand, which itself signals closeness and shared context.
  • Self-observation as humor. Framing one's own regret in third-person (後悔してる自分がいる) is a distinctly Japanese way of processing minor failure — it creates enough distance to laugh at yourself without dismissing the feeling entirely.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Grammar Point Core Meaning Emotional / Pragmatic Nuance Trigger Context JLPT なのに "Even though X, Y happened" Disappointment, frustration, irony, self-reproach — emotion is built into the form Contrast where speaker expected or hoped for a different outcome N3 のに "Even though X, Y" Same logic, slightly lighter emotional load — can be factual Factual contrast or mild surprise N4 けれど／けど "But / however" Neutral contrast — no emotional stance baked in General contrast, topic shift, soft contradiction N4 でも "But / even so" Casual neutral contrast or mild pushback Everyday speech, concession, changing subject N4 くせに "Even though / despite the fact that" Sharp, accusatory, contemptuous — directed at others Criticizing someone for acting above their station [N2 — awareness only] N2 それなのに "And yet / despite all that" Heavier than なのに — emphasizes the speaker's sense of betrayal or strong disappointment When the contrast feels particularly unfair or egregious N3

The closest lookalike is plain のに. The key difference is emotional intensity and attachment rules — なのに specifically marks noun and な-adjective predicates and carries a slightly stronger emotional charge. In casual speech they often feel interchangeable, but なのに on a noun predicate is structurally required, not stylistic.

docs.title: 〜けど / 〜けれど
docs.text:
The neutral alternative. Same logical contrast, no emotional loading. Reaches for this when you want to state a contrast without implying disappointment or reproach.

Example ダイエット中ちゅうだけどケーキ食たべた。 English "I'm on a diet but I ate cake." Intent Neutral statement of fact — no emotional texture vs. original Removes the irony and self-reproach entirely. Sounds matter-of-fact rather than wryly self-aware.

When to use this instead

Use けど when reporting a fact without wanting to colour it emotionally. If you're telling a doctor what you ate, けど fits. If you're laughing at yourself with a friend, なのに fits.

docs.title: 〜でも
docs.text:
Even more casual and lighter. Closer to "and yet" or "still" — doesn't flag the contrast as a violated expectation, just acknowledges it.

Example ダイエット中ちゅうでもケーキ食たべちゃった。 English "Even so, I went and ate cake while on my diet." Intent Casual acknowledgment — "I know, I know, and yet here we are" vs. original Lighter and slightly more resigned than なのに. Less self-aware, more simply stating the outcome.

docs.title: それなのに
docs.text:
A heavier, more emphatic version of なのに. The それ adds "despite all that / and yet even so" — raises the stakes of the contrast and signals stronger disappointment or a sense of betrayal.

Example あんなに頑張がんばったのに、それなのに結果けっかが出でなかった。 English "I worked so hard, and yet even with all that, the results didn't come." Intent Stronger emotional weight — the contrast feels particularly unfair vs. original Too heavy for casual self-directed irony. Reserve for situations where the contrast genuinely stings.

Learner trap — grammatically fine, emotionally wrong

Using それなのに for light everyday self-deprecation like the input sentence sounds over-dramatic. Native speakers would find it funny or slightly exhausting. なのに carries the right weight for minor personal ironies.

docs.title: くせに [N2 — awareness only]
docs.text:
Structurally similar to なのに but directed outward at others with contempt or accusation. Never use this for self-directed irony.

Example 知しらないくせに偉えらそうにしないで。 English "Don't act so superior when you don't even know anything." Intent Sharp reproach directed at another person — accusatory vs. original Completely different social function. なのに can turn inward; くせに cannot — it always points outward.

Learner trap — similar structure, opposite social function

Learners sometimes reach for くせに when they mean なのに because the logical structure looks similar. Using くせに in a self-directed sentence sounds grammatically wrong and socially bizarre to native speakers.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
The emotional nuance — wry self-reproach, mild irony — should be preserved across register shifts. The formality changes the packaging, not the feeling.

Casual Basic Polite Extra Polite

ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキ食たべちゃって…後悔こうかいしてる自分じぶんがいるよ。

"I'm on a diet and I went and ate cake anyway… there's definitely a part of me that regrets it."

Shift: Input sentence as-is. Casual speech, close friend register. ちゃって and trailing … are natural here.

ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキを食たべてしまって…後悔こうかいしている自分じぶんがいます。

"I'm on a diet but I ended up eating cake anyway… I find myself feeling rather regretful about it."

Shift: ちゃって→てしまって (undone contraction); いるよ→いています. The wry self-aware tone is preserved but the delivery is more composed. Suitable for acquaintances or colleagues.

ダイエット中ちゅうにもかかわらずケーキをいただいてしまいまして…少々しょうしょう後悔こうかいしております。

"Despite being on a diet, I found myself partaking in some cake… I must admit I'm feeling somewhat regretful."

Shift: にもかかわらず replaces なのに (formal written concessive); いただいてしまい uses humble receiving form; 後悔しております is humble progressive. The self-aware humor is largely lost — this sounds more like a formal apology than wry self-observation.

docs.title: Grammar Lineage
docs.text: Step Pattern What It Adds Example N4 — Foundation けど／けれど Neutral contrast connector — "but / however" 眠ねむいけど頑張がんばる。"I'm tired but I'll push through." N4 — Foundation のに Contrast with emotional weight — "even though, and yet" 勉強べんきょうしたのに落おちた。"I studied but still failed." N3 — Core なのに Adds な bridge for nouns and な-adjectives; same emotional weight as のに 休日きゅうじつなのに仕事しごとが入はいった。"It's my day off but work came in." N3 — Extended ← You are here なのに + inner monologue なのに embedded in self-aware narration of one's own contradictory behavior ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキ食たべちゃって…"I'm on a diet and I went and ate cake anyway…" N3 — Extended なのに sentence-final なのに used alone at sentence end as a pure emotional marker — complaint or reproach with no second clause 約束やくそくしたのに。"But you promised." N3 — Extended それなのに Emphatic variant — "and yet despite all that" — stronger sense of betrayal or unfairness あんなに頑張がんばったのに、それなのに…"I worked so hard, and yet even so…" N2 — Awareness くせに Same logical structure, sharper accusatory tone — directed outward only 知しらないくせに。"You don't even know anything." [N2 — awareness only]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
毎日まいにち練習れんしゅうしてるのに、なかなか上手うまくならない。 I practice every day and yet I just can't seem to improve. Literal: Every day practicing even-though, not-easily becoming-good.

~Context: Frustrated self-reflection — the speaker expected consistent practice to produce results. The disappointment is directed inward.~

昨日きのう早はやく寝ねたのに、まだ眠ねむい。 I went to bed early last night and I'm still tired. Literal: Yesterday early slept even-though, still sleepy.

~Context: Light complaint — the early bedtime was supposed to fix the tiredness. The contrast is mild and relatable.~

こんなに頑張がんばったのに、誰だれにも気きづいてもらえなかった。 I worked so hard and nobody even noticed. Literal: This-much worked-hard even-though, by-anyone noticed-not-received.

~Context: Genuine disappointment — the speaker put in effort and expected acknowledgment. Heavier emotional weight than the other examples.~

好すきなのに素直すなおになれなくて、ずっと意地いじを張はってた。 Even though I liked them, I couldn't be honest about it and just kept being stubborn. Literal: Liking even-though, honest-becoming unable, kept-stubbornly-holding-out.

~Context: Reflective confession — the speaker narrates their own contradictory behavior with self-awareness. Classic なのに pattern for emotional honesty between close friends or partners.~

晴れはれてるのに、なんか気分きぶんが上あがらない。 It's sunny out but somehow I just can't get into it. Literal: Sunny even-though, somehow mood not-rising.

~Context: Quiet introspective observation — the sunny weather was supposed to lift the mood but didn't. Gentle, melancholic tone.~

docs.title: 1. Forgetting な before のに with nouns and な-adjectives
docs.text:
The most common structural error. Learners attach のに directly to a noun without the required な, producing an ungrammatical sentence.

Incorrect

ダイエット[中のに]{.mistake}ケーキ食たべた。

~Missing な — ダイエット中 is a noun phrase and requires なのに, not のに.~

Correct

ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキ食たべた。

~な is required when attaching のに to a noun or な-adjective. Think of it as the same な in なので and なのだ.~

docs.title: 2. Using なのに to complain about yourself to your face to others
docs.text:
なのに is natural for self-directed irony when shared warmly with a close friend. Using it to complain about another person's behavior directly to their face — especially in polite contexts — can sound rude or aggressive.

Socially risky

あなたは大人おとななのにそんなこともできないの？

~"You're an adult and you can't even do that?" — confrontational, potentially humiliating. Fine between very close friends as teasing, but easily crosses a line.~

More natural

難むずかしいよね、こういうの。

~Redirect to shared empathy rather than direct reproach. Or use なのに only when the relationship clearly supports that level of directness.~

docs.title: 3. When NOT to Use It
docs.text:
Neutral factual contrast

When you simply want to state two facts that contrast without any emotional loading, なのに sounds inappropriately emotional. Use けど or が instead.

Avoid Use instead 今日きょうは月曜日げつようびなのに休やすみです。 "Today is Monday and it's a holiday." 今日きょうは月曜日げつようびですが休やすみです。 "Today is Monday, but it's a holiday."

なのに implies the Monday-ness was supposed to prevent it from being a holiday. が simply states both facts.

docs.title: Don't Confuse With
docs.text:
くせに — same logic, completely different social function

Both mark contrast between an established fact and a contradictory action. But くせに is accusatory and contemptuous — it always points outward at someone else, never inward at yourself.

Japanese Meaning ダイエット中ちゅうなのに食たべちゃった。 "I'm on a diet but I ate anyway." — self-directed, wry ダイエット中ちゅうのくせに食たべてる。 "You're on a diet and yet you're eating." — pointed accusation at another person

docs.title: Collocation Notes
docs.text:
Collocation Meaning ダイエット中ちゅうなのにケーキを食たべちゃった "I'm on a diet and I went and ate cake anyway" — the canonical self-directed irony chunk 約束やくそくしたのに "But you promised" / "Even though we made a promise" — sentence-final complaint marker, extremely common こんなに頑張がんばったのに "I worked this hard and yet…" — self-pity or frustrated effort with implied disappointing result 知しってるのに知しらないふりをする "Pretending not to know even though you know" — deliberate contradiction, slightly reproachful 晴れはれてるのになんか… "It's sunny but somehow…" — mood mismatch opener, melancholic introspection

~These collocations appear frequently enough that learning them as complete chunks is more useful than assembling them from parts each time.~

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• なのに can trail off completely. ダイエット中なのに… with nothing following is a complete and natural utterance in casual speech. The second clause is implied by the situation and the listener's understanding. This trailing use is far more common in real conversation than the complete two-clause textbook version.
  • ちゃった and なのに are a natural pair. てしまう (and its casual contraction ちゃう) expresses accidental or regrettable completion — something that happened somewhat against one's will or better judgment. Combined with なのに the sentence packages both the violated expectation (なのに) and the helpless quality of the violation (ちゃった) in a single breath.
  • 後悔してる自分がいる is a distinct construction. "There exists a self that regrets" uses 自分がいる to create mild self-objectification — the speaker steps slightly outside themselves to observe their own emotion. This softens the confession and invites the listener to laugh along rather than offer sympathy. It's a recurring pattern in casual Japanese self-disclosure worth learning as its own chunk.
  • Sentence-final のに as a standalone complaint — 約束やくそくしたのに。— has no second clause at all. The complaint is the trailing expectation left hanging. The more the second clause is implied rather than stated, the more emotional weight the sentence carries. This is a case where saying less is saying more.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text: Stage What It Looks Like Now — N3 Recognize なのに reliably in speech; understand the emotional contrast function; produce complete two-clause sentences correctly with the な rule applied; understand the input sentence fully Developing — N3→N2 Start using the trailing form naturally (なのに…); begin distinguishing when to use なのに vs. けど based on emotional intent rather than grammar rules; feel the difference between self-directed and other-directed use Automatic — N2+ なのに arrives as an emotional signal before the second clause — you hear the disappointment or irony before the full sentence lands; the trailing form feels as natural as the complete form Mastery You deploy the full emotional register — wry self-awareness, soft reproach, quiet irony — without consciously selecting the pattern; you also know when a situation calls for neutral けど instead, not because of a rule but because なのに would feel too loaded

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• のに — the base form; lighter emotional weight; attaches to verb and adjective plain forms directly
  • それなのに — emphatic variant; stronger sense of betrayal or unfairness
  • なのだから／なんだから — explains or justifies rather than contrasts; "precisely because X, therefore Y"

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• くせに — same logical structure, accusatory and contemptuous; directed outward only [N2 — awareness only]
  • けど／けれど — neutral contrast; no emotional stance
  • でも — casual concession; "even so / but still"

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• のに (N4) — base contrast pattern with emotional weight
  • な-adjective + な linking form (N4) — the grammar behind why だ becomes な before のに
  • てしまう／ちゃう (N4) — regrettable completion; pairs naturally with なのに

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• くせに (N2) — sharper, accusatory variant; worth recognizing
  • にもかかわらず (N2) — formal written equivalent of なのに; appears in essays and formal speech [N2 — awareness only]
  • ながらも (N2) — "even while / despite simultaneously" — concurrent contrast rather than sequential [N2 — awareness only]

docs.title: 1. Overview & Nuance
docs.text:
An embedded question is a question tucked inside a larger sentence, functioning as a noun phrase — the subject or object of the main verb. In English, we do this with words like "whether" or "if":

  • "I don't know whether she will come." (whether-clause is the object of "know")
  • "Let's decide where to eat." (where-clause is the object of "decide")
  • "I'm wondering if the meeting is tomorrow." (if-clause is the object of "wondering")

Japanese handles this with two closely related patterns:

Pattern A — Wh-Question Embedded

Plain-form clause containing 何なに/誰だれ/どこ/いつ/etc. + か + main verb

Pattern B — Yes/No Question Embedded

Plain-form clause + かどうか + main verb

The difference is exactly the same as in English: 〜か embeds wh-questions (what / who / where / when / why / how), while 〜かどうか embeds yes/no questions (whether or not).

Why this pattern is worth mastering

Embedded questions are one of the most common structures in natural Japanese — you will hear them constantly in sentences like「どこに行いくか決きめた?」("Did you decide where to go?") or「彼かれが来くるかどうか分わからない。」("I don't know whether he's coming.") Mastering them unlocks a big chunk of natural conversation.

docs.title: 2a. The Plain-Form Requirement
docs.text:
The clause inside the embedded question must use plain form, not polite form. This is the biggest mistake learners make — they try to tuck 〜ます forms into embedded questions.

Part of Speech Form Inside Embedded Q Example Verbs Plain form (dictionary / plain past / plain negative) 行いく、行いった、行いかない い-adjectives Plain form (unchanged) 高たかい、高たかかった、高たかくない な-adjectives Drop だ before か / かどうか 元気げんき (not 元気げんきだ) Nouns Drop だ before か / かどうか 学生がくせい (not 学生がくせいだ)

The 'dropping だ' rule

This is the single most common grammatical trip-up in this pattern. When a na-adjective or noun comes before か or かどうか, you drop the だ:

✅ 彼かれが学生がくせいかどうか知しりません。 ❌ 彼かれが学生がくせいだかどうか知しりません。

However, for past tense or negative, the copula conjugation stays: 学生がくせいだったかどうか / 学生がくせいじゃないかどうか are both correct.

docs.title: 2b. Pattern A — 〜か (Embedded Wh-Questions)
docs.text:
Use 〜か when the embedded question contains a question word: 何なに、誰だれ、どこ、いつ、どう、どうして、どの、どれ、いくら、いくつ, etc.

Original Question Embedded Version 何なにを食たべますか。 何なにを食たべるか決きめましょう。 どこに住すんでいますか。 どこに住すんでいるか教おしえてください。 誰だれが来きましたか。 誰だれが来きたか分わかりません。 いつ始はじまりますか。 いつ始はじまるか聞ききましょう。

docs.title: 2c. Pattern B — 〜かどうか (Embedded Yes/No Questions)
docs.text:
Use 〜かどうか when the embedded question is a yes/no question — one with no question word. The どうか part literally means "or how (i.e., or not)," so かどうか = "whether or not."

Original Question Embedded Version 彼かれは来きますか。 彼かれが来くるかどうか分わかりません。 この店みせは開あいていますか。 この店みせが開あいているかどうか確たしかめます。 これで正ただしいですか。 これで正ただしいかどうか教おしえてください。 彼かれは元気げんきですか。 彼かれが元気げんきかどうかわかりません。

The particle shift: は → が

Notice that は often becomes が when a question gets embedded. This is because the embedded clause is no longer the "topic" of the whole sentence — it's a subordinate clause, and subordinate clauses strongly prefer が for marking their subjects. Not a strict rule, but a strong tendency to internalize.

docs.title: 2d. Main-Verb Pairings
docs.text:
Embedded questions are always the object or subject of some main verb. These are the verbs you'll see 90% of the time:

Main Verb Meaning Use Case 分わかる / 知しる to understand / know Expressing lack of knowledge 決きめる to decide Making a decision about something open 聞きく to ask / hear Inquiring or being told 教おしえる to tell / teach Requesting information 考かんがえる to think / consider Deliberating 覚おぼえている / 忘わすれる to remember / forget Memory 調しらべる / 確たしかめる to check / verify Investigation 心配しんぱいする to worry Expressing anxiety about uncertainty

docs.title: Level 1 — Basic Daily Use
docs.text:
Example 1 · Making Plans

週末しゅうまつ、旅行りょこうに行いくかどうか決きめましょう。 "Let's decide whether or not we'll go on a trip this weekend."

Yes/no question (go or not go), so かどうか. The main verb 決きめる takes the embedded question as its object.

Example 2 · Asking for Directions

駅えきがどこにあるか教おしえてください。 "Please tell me where the station is."

Wh-question (どこ), so plain か. Note が marking the subject inside the embedded clause.

Example 3 · Forgetting Something

彼かれの名前なまえが何なんだったか忘わすれました。 "I forgot what his name was."

Past tense inside the embedded clause: 何なんだった uses the past form of the copula. The だ isn't dropped here because it's だった (past), not だ (present).

docs.title: Level 2 — Expressing Uncertainty
docs.text:
Example 4 · Checking Availability

その映画えいががまだ上映じょうえいしているかどうか調しらべてみます。 "I'll check whether that movie is still showing."

Example 5 · Self-Doubt

この仕事しごとが自分じぶんに合あっているかどうかわかりません。 "I don't know whether this job suits me."

Example 6 · Polite Inquiry

この料理りょうりに肉にくが入はいっているかどうか教おしえていただけますか。 "Could you tell me whether this dish contains meat?"

docs.title: Level 3 — Business & Formal Usage
docs.text:
Example 7 · Business Email

来週らいしゅうの会議かいぎにご出席しゅっせきいただけるかどうか、お知しらせください。 "Please let us know whether you'll be able to attend next week's meeting."

Standard scheduling phrasing for business correspondence.

Example 8 · Project Decision

このプロジェクトを続つづけるかどうか、来月らいげつまでに決定けっていする必要ひつようがあります。 "We need to decide by next month whether to continue this project."

Example 9 · Nested Question

なぜ彼かれが辞やめたか誰だれも知しらないようです。 "It seems no one knows why he quit."

The embedded question (なぜ彼かれが辞やめたか) is the object of 知しる, wrapped in 〜ようです (it seems).

docs.title: Level 4 — Advanced Nuance
docs.text:
Example 10 · Hedged Opinion

この方法ほうほうが正ただしいかどうかは別べつとして、まずやってみましょう。 "Setting aside whether this method is correct, let's first give it a try."

The fixed phrase 〜かどうかは別べつとして ("setting aside whether...") is an elegant way to acknowledge uncertainty while pushing forward.

docs.title: 4. 〜か vs. 〜かどうか — Choosing the Right One
docs.text:
The choice is entirely determined by whether the embedded clause contains a question word:

Does the Embedded Clause Have a Question Word? Use English Parallel ✅ Yes — 何なに、誰だれ、どこ、いつ、etc. 〜か what / who / where / when / why / how ❌ No — just a yes/no question 〜かどうか whether (or not) / if

docs.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
docs.text: Meaning Japanese Pattern "I don't know where he went." 彼かれがどこに行いったかわかりません。 〜か (wh) "I don't know whether he went." 彼かれが行いったかどうかわかりません。 〜かどうか (y/n) "Please tell me when the meeting starts." 会議かいぎがいつ始はじまるか教おしえてください。 〜か (wh) "Please tell me whether the meeting is starting." 会議かいぎが始はじまるかどうか教おしえてください。 〜かどうか (y/n) "Let's decide what to eat." 何なにを食たべるか決きめましょう。 〜か (wh) "Let's decide whether to eat dinner out." 外食がいしょくするかどうか決きめましょう。 〜かどうか (y/n)

docs.title: 5. The Mistake Monitor
docs.text:
Pitfall 1 — Using Polite Form Inside the Embedded Clause

The clause inside 〜か or 〜かどうか must be in plain form, even if the main verb is polite.

❌ 彼かれが来きますかどうかわかりません。 ✅ 彼かれが来くるかどうかわかりません。

The politeness level is set by the main verb (わかりません), not the embedded clause. The embedded clause is subordinate and always uses plain form.

Pitfall 2 — Forgetting to Drop だ

With na-adjectives and nouns in present affirmative, drop the だ before か or かどうか:

❌ 彼かれが学生がくせいだかどうか… ✅ 彼かれが学生がくせいかどうか…

❌ その町まちが静しずかだかどうか… ✅ その町まちが静しずかかどうか…

This rule applies only to present affirmative. Past tense (だった) and negative (じゃない / ではない) forms keep their shape.

Pitfall 3 — Using 〜かどうか with a Question Word

The どうか part means "or not" — it's meaningless when there's already a question word like "what" or "where."

❌ 何なにを食たべるかどうか決きめましょう。 (nonsensical — "Let's decide whether to eat what") ✅ 何なにを食たべるか決きめましょう。 ("Let's decide what to eat")

Mental check: wh-question → use か. Whether/if → use かどうか.

Stylistic Tip — Dropping the Final か in Casual Speech

In casual speech, the final か in an embedded wh-question is sometimes dropped with verbs like 知しっている / 分わかる:

Formal: 彼かれがどこに住すんでいるか、知しってる? Casual: 彼かれがどこに住すんでる、知しってる?

The か-dropped version sounds rough. For N3 study and any formal register, keep the か.

Nuance — 〜かどうか vs. 〜か〜ないか

There's an alternative form that spells out the "or not" explicitly:

行いくかどうか分わからない ≈ 行いくか行いかないか分わからない

"I don't know whether I'll go or not."

The 〜か〜ないか version is slightly more emphatic about the binary choice. Both are natural; かどうか is more common and compact.

docs.title: 6. The Comparison Table
docs.text: Scenario N4: Direct Question N3: Embedded Question Nuance Difference Knowing 何時なんじですか？ "What time is it?" 何時なんじか知しっていますか。 "Do you know what time it is?" Embedded is much softer — better for strangers or superiors. Checking 雨あめが降ふりますか。 "Will it rain?" 雨あめが降ふるかどうか確たしかめます。 "I will check if it will rain." Turns the question into an object of investigation. Deciding 結婚けっこんしますか？ "Will you marry?" 結婚けっこんするか考かんがえています。 "I'm thinking about whether to marry." Allows you to express internal process and deliberation.

docs.title: 7. Practice Drill
docs.text:
1. Convert: 「会議かいぎは何時なんじに始はじまりますか？」→ "Please tell me what time the meeting starts."
  2. Combine: この答こたえは正ただしいですか。+ 確たしかめてください。→ One sentence using 〜か or 〜かどうか.
  3. Translate: "I haven't decided yet whether I'll study abroad next year."
  4. Translate: "Do you remember where we put the keys?"
  5. Spot and fix the error: 彼かれが元気げんきだかどうか心配しんぱいしています。

docs.title: 8. Answer Key
docs.text:
Click to reveal answers

Question 1 会議かいぎが何時なんじに始はじまるか教おしえてください。 Wh-question (何時なんじ), so use 〜か. Note the particle shift from は to が: the embedded clause is subordinate and takes が for its subject. The polite form 始はじまります becomes plain 始はじまる.

Question 2 この答こたえが正ただしいかどうか確たしかめてください。 Yes/no question (is it correct?), so use 〜かどうか. 正ただしい is an i-adjective, so it attaches directly with no だ issue.

Question 3 来年らいねん留学りゅうがくするかどうか、まだ決きめていません。 Yes/no question (will study abroad or not), so 〜かどうか. 決きめていません (have not decided) is the natural form — not 決きめません (won't decide), because the issue is ongoing indecision, not refusal.

Question 4 鍵かぎをどこに置おいたか覚おぼえていますか。 Wh-question (どこ), so 〜か. Past tense 置おいた preserved inside the embedded clause.

Question 5 ❌ 彼かれが元気げんきだかどうか心配しんぱいしています。 ✅ 彼かれが元気げんきかどうか心配しんぱいしています。 元気げんき is a na-adjective; the だ must be dropped before かどうか in present affirmative form. Compare to past tense: 元気げんきだったかどうか is fine because だった is past-tense copula, not present だ.

docs.title: あっという間に vs. いつの間にか — Subjective Speed vs. Unnoticed Change
あっという間まに vs. 何時いつの間まにか — "In the Blink of an Eye" vs. "Before I Knew It"
JLPT Level
docs.text:
Both expressions appear in N3-N2 materials, but native-like command of their distinct emotional textures is an N2-level skill. Learners at the Lower N3 stage can recognize both, but deploying each appropriately requires understanding the speaker's relationship to the event — were you watching it happen, or did you miss it entirely?

Tier Expression Core Meaning Learner Status N3 Recognition あっという間まに Subjective speed: the event felt shockingly fast to the speaker Recognizable from listening and reading N3 Recognition 何時いつの間まにか Unnoticed transition: the change happened without the speaker realizing Recognizable from listening and reading N2 Mastery Both Distinguishing the two in real-time production and choosing the right emotional frame Requires active practice and feedback

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
• あっという間まに means "in the blink of an eye" — the speaker was watching the clock and is surprised by how fast it moved.
  • 何時いつの間まにか means "before I knew it" or "without my noticing" — the speaker wasn't watching at all, and the change snuck up on them.
  • The core distinction is presence of awareness during the event: あっという間まに implies you were there but time flew; 何時いつの間まにか implies you looked up and the world had already shifted.
  • Mixing these up produces sentences that are grammatically correct but emotionally wrong — a native listener will feel the disconnect.

docs.title: あっという間まに
docs.text:
The speaker experienced the passage of time as startlingly fast. The event felt compressed — you blinked and it was over. This expression always carries a subjective, emotional reaction to speed: surprise, wistfulness, or mild shock.

Japanese English 夏休なつやすみはあっという間まに終おわってしまった。 Summer vacation was over in the blink of an eye. Literal: Summer vacation, in-a-startlingly-short-moment, ended up finishing. The speaker felt the time pass — and was shocked by how fast it went.

docs.title: 何時いつの間まにか
docs.text:
The speaker did not perceive the transition at all. A change occurred during a gap in attention — the speaker looked away, and when they looked back, the state had shifted. The emphasis is on unconscious or unnoticed transformation, not on subjective speed.

Japanese English 何時いつの間まにか外そとが暗くらくなっていた。 Before I knew it, it had gotten dark outside. Literal: At-some-point-without-my-noticing, outside had become dark. The speaker wasn't tracking the sunset — they looked up and it was already dark.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: The difference between these two expressions is not about how much time actually passed. It's about where the speaker's attention was during that time.

docs.title: あっという間まに: Watching the Clock
docs.text:
The phrase literally means "in the space of saying 'ah!'" — the duration of a single surprised breath. The speaker was present and attentive, but the experience felt compressed. Time didn't sneak by; it rushed by, and the speaker noticed it rushing.

This expression often carries a specific emotional color. The speaker feels a small loss — something enjoyable ended too quickly, or a deadline arrived before they were ready. The surprise can be positive, rueful, or bittersweet, but it always contains the sense: "I was there, and I still can't believe how fast that was."

It pairs naturally with verbs describing bounded events that the speaker experienced directly: a vacation ending, a year passing, a meal being finished, a performance concluding.

docs.title: 何時いつの間まにか: Looking Away
docs.text:
The phrase literally means "in the gap of 'when?'" — the speaker can't identify the moment of change because they weren't tracking it. Unlike the fast-time expression, this one is about an unnoticed boundary. The speaker was absorbed in something else, or the change was too gradual to register in real time.

The emotional color here is often mild surprise with a touch of disorientation. You look up and the world has reconfigured itself while you weren't paying attention. This can be neutral (the weather changed), poignant (a child grew up), or unsettling (a relationship drifted apart).

It pairs naturally with verbs describing state changes: getting dark, becoming an adult, falling asleep, a situation having shifted.

docs.title: Direct Comparison
docs.text: Scenario あっという間まに 何時いつの間まにか A three-hour movie you loved The movie felt like it was over in a flash — you were riveted the whole time. You weren't really paying attention to the movie. A child growing taller You watched them every day and still can't believe how fast it went. You visited after a year away and suddenly they're taller than you. A work deadline You were working steadily and suddenly it's 5 PM. You got distracted by something and the deadline arrived while you weren't looking. Rain starting You were watching the sky darken and then it poured — fast. You were deep in conversation and looked up — the ground was already wet.

docs.title: Structural Breakdown
docs.text:
あっという間まに

  • あっ: a single-syllable exclamation of surprise (variant of あ)
  • という: quotative "called" / "that one says"
  • 間ま: interval, space of time
  • に: particle marking the time frame in which something occurs

The whole phrase is a set expression functioning as an adverb. It cannot be productively modified — you cannot say 長ながいという間まに or 驚おどろくという間まに.

何時いつの間まにか

  • 何時いつ: "when" / "what time"
  • の: possessive / attributive
  • 間ま: interval, gap, space between
  • に: particle marking the time frame
  • か: question marker, here fossilized into "at some unknown point"

The literal structure is "in the interval of when? + か" — the か turns the unknown into a vague existential: "at some moment I can't identify." Unlike あっという間まに, this expression has a productive variant: 気きがつけば (if I notice / when I came to) works similarly and can replace it in many contexts.

docs.title: Usage Patterns
docs.text: Pattern Structure Example あっという間まに + past tense Subjective speed frame + completed event あっという間まに一週間いっしゅうかんが過すぎた。 何時いつの間まにか + 〜ていた Unnoticed change + ongoing/resultant state 何時いつの間まにか眠ねむっていた。

docs.title: Japanese Mindset
docs.text:
Both expressions reflect a Japanese attentiveness to the speaker's own perceptual experience — the grammar doesn't just report what happened, it encodes how the speaker experienced it. English handles this through word choice and intonation, but Japanese bakes it into fixed adverbial expressions.

  • あっという間まに encodes emotional time, not calendar time. A minute can feel like an hour, or a year can feel like a day. This expression affirms the speaker's felt experience over objective measurement. It's a small way of saying: my perception matters.
  • 何時いつの間まにか encodes humble inattention. The speaker admits, without shame, that they weren't tracking. This is not an apology — it's an acknowledgment that attention is finite and the world moves without us. There's a quiet, sometimes poignant acceptance in it.
  • Neither expression translates cleanly to English temporal logic. "In the blink of an eye" comes close for the first, and "before I knew it" for the second, but the English versions don't carry the same internal structure — English relies on metaphor, Japanese on perceptual framing.

docs.title: Nuance Comparison
docs.text:
Expression Core Meaning Speaker's Attention During Event Emotional Color Typical Verb Types あっという間まに "In a startlingly short time" Present and watching Surprise, mild loss, wistfulness Bounded events: 終わる, 過ぎる, 無くなる 何時いつの間まにか "Without my noticing" Absent or distracted Mild disorientation, poignancy State changes: 〜くなる, 〜ていた, 〜てしまう 瞬またたく間まに "In the time it takes to blink" Present and watching Pure speed, more neutral than あっという間に Same as あっという間に but more formal/written 気きがついたら "When I came to / realized" Absent, then awareness arrives Active realization, sometimes alarmed Same as いつの間にか but with a stronger "wake-up" moment いつの間まにか Same as 何時いつの間まにか; slightly more casual Same Same Same

瞬またたく間まに is the most frequent source of confusion for learners who encounter it in reading. It belongs to the same family as あっという間まに (the speaker was watching time fly) but is slightly more formal and less emotionally charged — it reports speed without necessarily reporting surprise.

docs.title: Register and Formality
docs.text:
Casual

  • あっという間まに休やすみ終おわっちゃったね。

"Break went by so fast, huh."

  • いつの間まにか雨あめ降ふってた。

"Somehow it started raining without me noticing."

Both expressions are fully natural in casual conversation. 何時いつの間まにか commonly contracts to いつの間まにか in casual speech — this is not a grammatical contraction but a fixed colloquial variant. Both forms are equally casual; いつの間まにか is slightly faster in speech.

Polite

  • あっという間まに一週間いっしゅうかんが過すぎました。

"A week passed in the blink of an eye."

  • 何時いつの間まにか外そとが暗くらくなっていました。

"Before I realized it, it had gotten dark outside."

Both expressions accept です・ます without any structural adjustment. The emotional content is unchanged — politeness here is purely grammatical, not pragmatic.

Written / Formal

  • 瞬またたく間まに時ときが過すぎ去った。

"Time flew past in the blink of an eye."

  • 気きがつけば、季節きせつはすっかり移うつろっていた。

"Before I knew it, the season had completely changed."

In formal writing, あっという間まに is frequently replaced by 瞬またたく間まに. 何時いつの間まにか is often replaced by 気きがつけば or いつの間まにか in narrative prose. These are stylistic choices, not register requirements — both original expressions can appear in formal writing without sounding wrong.

docs.title: あっという間まに
docs.text: Japanese English 子こどもの成長せいちょうはあっという間まに過すぎていく。 Children grow up in the blink of an eye. Literal: Children's growth, in-a-startlingly-short-moment, passes by. A parent who watches their child every day and is still shocked by how fast it goes. The speaker is present for the process but amazed by the speed. 楽たのしい時間じかんはあっという間まに終おわってしまう。 Fun times are over before you know it. Literal: Enjoyable time, in-a-startlingly-short-moment, ends up finishing. The universal experience of pleasure compressing perceived time. The speaker is fully engaged and grieving the speed. あっという間まに締切しめきが来きてしまった。 The deadline was here in the blink of an eye. Literal: In-a-startlingly-short-moment, the deadline ended up coming. The speaker was working and aware of the approaching deadline, but it still arrived faster than expected. There's rueful surprise here.

docs.title: 何時いつの間まにか
docs.text: Japanese English 何時いつの間まにか日ひが暮くれていた。 Before I knew it, the sun had set. Literal: At-some-point-without-my-noticing, day had fallen. The speaker was absorbed in something indoors and didn't track the light fading. They looked up and it was dark. 何時いつの間まにか彼かれと話はなさなくなっていた。 Before I realized it, I'd stopped talking to him. Literal: At-some-point-without-my-noticing, I had come to not talk with him. A relationship that drifted apart through inattention, not conflict. The speaker didn't decide to stop — it just happened in the gaps. 何時いつの間まにか涙なみだが出でていた。 Before I knew it, tears were streaming down my face. Literal: At-some-point-without-my-noticing, tears had come out. The speaker was so emotionally overwhelmed that they didn't register the physical response until it was already happening. A powerful use of this expression that captures dissociation during intense feeling.

docs.title: Contrastive Pair
docs.text: Japanese English あっという間まに雨あめが降ふり出だした。 It went from nothing to pouring in a flash. The speaker was watching the sky, saw it darken, and was surprised by how fast the rain arrived. 何時いつの間まにか雨あめが降ふっていた。 Somehow it had started raining without my noticing. The speaker was absorbed in something else and looked up to find the ground already wet.

docs.title: Using あっという間まに for an unnoticed state change
docs.text:
Learners who treat both expressions as general "suddenly" equivalents will use あっという間まに for situations where the speaker was clearly not paying attention — a sunset while working, a child growing up while the parent was away. The result is grammatically fine but emotionally misaligned.

Incorrect:

仕事しごとに集中しゅうちゅうしていて、あっという間まに外そとが暗くらくなっていた。

"I was focused on work, and in the blink of an eye it had gotten dark outside." — Contradictory: "focused on work" means you weren't tracking time, but あっという間まに claims you were.

Correct:

仕事しごとに集中しゅうちゅうしていて、何時いつの間まにか外そとが暗くらくなっていた。

"I was focused on work, and before I knew it, it had gotten dark outside." — Consistent: you weren't watching, and the change snuck up on you.

docs.title: Using 何時いつの間まにか for an experience the speaker was fully present for
docs.text:
The reverse error: using the unnoticed-change expression for something the speaker experienced directly and found surprisingly fast.

Incorrect:

楽たのしくて、何時いつの間まにかコンサートが終おわっていた。

"It was so fun, and before I knew it the concert had ended." — Contradictory: "so fun" means you were present and engaged, but 何時いつの間まにか claims you didn't notice the transition.

Correct:

楽たのしくて、あっという間まにコンサートが終おわってしまった。

"It was so fun, and the concert was over in the blink of an eye." — Consistent: you were engaged and the time flew.

docs.title: Don't Confuse With
docs.text:
突然とつぜん / 急きゅうに

Neither あっという間まに nor 何時いつの間まにか means "suddenly" in the sense of an abrupt external event. 突然とつぜん and 急きゅうに describe the event itself as abrupt. あっという間まに and 何時いつの間まにか describe the speaker's perceptual experience of the event.

Expression Focus Example 突然とつぜん The event itself was abrupt 突然とつぜん、停電ていでんした。 "Suddenly, the power went out." あっという間まに The speaker experienced the duration as startlingly fast あっという間まに日ひが暮くれた。 "The sun set in a flash (I was watching and it surprised me)." 何時いつの間まにか The speaker didn't notice the transition at all 何時いつの間まにか日ひが暮くれていた。 "The sun had set without my noticing."

docs.title: Hidden Points
docs.text:
• あっという間まに can carry mild grief. The expression is not neutral about speed — it often signals that the speaker wishes the time had been longer. A vacation that ends あっという間まに is a vacation the speaker misses. Using it for something unpleasant (a boring meeting) would be strange — you'd reach for やっと or ようやく instead.
  • 何時いつの間まにか can carry quiet alarm. When the unnoticed change is negative — a friendship faded, a health problem developed — the expression carries an undercurrent of "I should have been paying attention." It's not an apology, but it is an acknowledgment of inattention with consequences.
  • The 〜ていた ending is near-obligatory with 何時いつの間まにか. Because the speaker discovers the change after it has already occurred, the resultant-state 〜ていた form is the natural pairing. Using simple past 〜た is not incorrect but loses the sense of looking at the aftermath.
  • あっという間まに can pair with both 〜た and 〜てしまう. The 〜てしまう form amplifies the emotional content — it adds a sense of "it ended up happening (and I have feelings about that)." This is the most natural form when the event was something the speaker wanted to last longer.

docs.title: Learner Stage
docs.text:
• What you can do now: Recognize both expressions in listening and reading and understand their distinct emotional frames. You can produce each in simple, controlled sentences with familiar verbs — especially the common collocations with 終おわる, 過すぎる, 暗くらくなる, and 眠ねむる.
  • What still takes effort: Choosing the right expression in real-time conversation when the situation is ambiguous — when you were half-watching, half-distracted, and the experience was a mix of speed and inattention. Monitoring whether your verb choice (終おわった vs. 終おわっていた) aligns with the expression you chose also requires conscious attention at this stage.
  • What comes later: The distinction becomes automatic through exposure to narrative Japanese — novels, interviews, podcast storytelling — where native speakers move fluidly between these frames depending on what they want you to feel about the passage of time. You'll stop calculating and start feeling which one belongs.

docs.title: Same Family
docs.text:
• 瞬またたく間まに — "In the blink of an eye" (more formal/literary sibling of あっという間まに)
  • 気きがついたら — "When I came to / realized" (stronger wake-up moment sibling of 何時いつの間まにか)
  • いつの間まにか — Casual contracted form of 何時いつの間まにか

docs.title: Commonly Confused With
docs.text:
• 突然とつぜん / 急きゅうに — "Suddenly" (objective abruptness of the event, not the speaker's perceptual experience)
  • あっという間まだった — Predicate form: "It was over in a flash" (sentence-final summary, not an adverbial)

docs.title: Builds From
docs.text:
• 〜てしまう (N4) — Completion/regret auxiliary, natural pairing with あっという間まに
  • 〜ていた (N4) — Resultant state, natural pairing with 何時いつの間まにか
  • 間ま as "interval" (N4) — Core noun in both expressions

docs.title: Unlocks Next
docs.text:
• 知しらぬ間まに — "Without one's knowing" (more formal/literary variant) [N2 — awareness only]
  • いつの間まにやら — "Before I knew it (emphatic, slightly folksy)" [N2 — awareness only]

docs.title: て-form
docs.text: The te-form is one of the most productive forms in Japanese — it connects clauses, makes requests, signals permission and prohibition, and stacks with dozens of auxiliaries.
